entries.description: Extract and update translatable strings in Microsoft .NET RESX 2.0 and .resw resource files.

schema.title: .NET RESX
schema.description: Configuration for the native .NET RESX / .resw (Microsoft ResX 2.0) reader/writer

extractComments.title: Extract comments as notes
extractComments.description: Surface each string <data> entry's sibling <comment> element as a translator note on the emitted block. Comments always round-trip verbatim regardless of this setting.

skipNameDataReferences.title: Skip name-reference entries
skipNameDataReferences.description: Exclude designer name-reference entries (those whose name starts with ">", e.g. ">>control.Name") from extraction. These carry WinForms field names, not UI strings.

doc.overview:
The **.NET RESX** format reads Microsoft ResX 2.0 resource files and their
string-only `.resw` variant — the standard localization container for .NET
applications (locale by satellite filename, e.g. `Strings.fr.resx`).

String `<data>` entries become translatable blocks: the `name` attribute is
the block name, `<value>` is the source, a sibling `<comment>` becomes a
translator note, and `xml:space="preserve"` is honored. .NET composite-format
placeholders such as `{0}` and `{1:t}` are protected as inline codes. Typed
and binary `<data>` (those carrying `type`/`mimetype`), `<metadata>`,
`<assembly>`, name-reference entries, and the `<resheader>` boilerplate pass
through untouched. Round-trips are byte-faithful — only changed `<value>`
content is spliced back into the original document.

extractComments.description: Attach each entry's <comment> as a translator note.
extractComments.help:
When enabled (the default), a string entry's sibling `<comment>` element
is attached to the block as a translator note. Disable to ignore
comments.

skipNameDataReferences.help:
When enabled (the default), `<data>` entries whose name begins with `>`
(file/resource references rather than inline string values) are not
extracted.

processingNotes: Only string `<data>` entries are translatable; typed/binary data, metadata, assembly, and resheader entries pass through untouched.
`xml:space="preserve"` on an entry sets the block's preserve-whitespace flag.
.NET composite-format placeholders (`{0}`, `{1:t}`) are protected as inline codes.

examples.title: Ignore comments
examples.description: Do not turn <comment> elements into translator notes.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from Adobe FrameMaker Maker Interchange Format (MIF) documents.

doc.overview:
MIF (Maker Interchange Format) is Adobe FrameMaker's text-based interchange
format. A MIF file is a brace-delimited, SGML-flavoured statement tree
introduced by a version header line (`<MIFFile 2015>`, `<MIFFile 9.00>`, …);
translatable text lives inside `<String \`…'>` values nested under `<Para>`
paragraphs that hang off text-flow, table, and note containers.

neokapi reads MIF and writes it back, preserving the surrounding statement
structure as skeleton so untranslated markup round-trips unchanged. By
default it extracts paragraph text from body, master, reference, and hidden
pages, along with variable definitions and index-marker text. Hyperlink
text, cross-reference format strings, and paragraph-numbering format strings
are left out by default. A regex-based code finder protects FrameMaker
auto-numbering prefixes and Asian numbering format names from being altered
during processing such as pseudo-translation. These extraction toggles
mirror the upstream Okapi MIF filter parameters; the native reader applies
the same defaults but does not expose them as a configurable JSON schema in
this reference.
limitations: Hyperlink text, cross-reference format strings, and paragraph-numbering format strings are not extracted by default.
Extraction toggles match the upstream Okapi MIF filter but are not surfaced as configurable schema properties in this reference.
processingNotes: Body, master, reference, and hidden page content is extracted by default; variables and index markers are also extracted.
Non-translatable MIF statements are preserved as skeleton for byte-faithful round-trips.
A code finder protects FrameMaker auto-numbering prefixes (`H:`, `T:`, …) and Asian numbering format names from being treated as translatable text.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from Adobe InDesign IDML packages.

doc.overview:
IDML (InDesign Markup Language) is the XML-based interchange format Adobe
InDesign produces. An `.idml` file is a ZIP package (UCF) containing a
`designmap.xml` index plus one or more `Stories/Story_*.xml` sub-documents.
neokapi walks each story in spread order, extracting translatable text from
`<Content>` runs while preserving the surrounding XML — paragraph and
character style ranges, hyperlink sources, and the package structure — as
skeleton.

neokapi reads and writes IDML, round-tripping the package so the translated
output remains a valid InDesign document. By default it extracts text from
master spreads but skips footnote and endnote text, content on hidden
InDesign layers, and items hidden on the pasteboard; soft (discretionary)
hyphens are kept. These choices mirror the upstream Okapi IDML filter
defaults. The native reader applies the same defaults but does not expose
them as a configurable JSON schema in this reference.
limitations: Footnote and endnote text, hidden-layer stories, and hidden pasteboard items are not extracted by default.
Extraction toggles match the upstream Okapi IDML filter but are not surfaced as configurable schema properties in this reference.
processingNotes: Stories are gathered from `<TextFrame>` / `<TextPath>` elements in spread order; each sub-document is treated independently.
Master-spread text is extracted by default; the surrounding XML is preserved as skeleton for round-trips.
On writing, `HyperlinkTextSource` content is always reconstructed as inline elements in the story XML.

entries.description: Extract and update translatable strings in Android string resource files (res/values/strings.xml).

schema.title: Android String Resources
schema.description: Configuration for the native Android string-resources (res/values/strings.xml) reader/writer

extractComments.title: Extract comments as notes
extractComments.description: Surface an XML comment immediately preceding an entry as a translator note on the emitted block. Comments always round-trip verbatim regardless of this setting.

skipNonTranslatable.title: Skip translatable="false" entries
skipNonTranslatable.description: Exclude <string>/<string-array>/<plurals> entries marked translatable="false" from extraction. Such resources are developer-owned and round-trip verbatim.

skipResourceReferences.title: Skip resource references
skipResourceReferences.description: Exclude <string> values that are a bare resource reference (e.g. @string/foo, ?attr/bar). A reference is an alias, not translatable UI text.

doc.overview:
The **Android String Resources** format reads `res/values/strings.xml`
files with full localization awareness, rather than as generic XML. It
extracts `<string>`, `<string-array>` items, and `<plurals>` items
(by CLDR `quantity`) as translatable blocks.

Android l10n semantics are honored: entries marked `translatable="false"`
and bare resource references (`@string/foo`, `?attr/bar`) are skipped;
positional/printf arguments (`%1$s`, `%d`, `%%`), `<xliff:g>` do-not-translate
spans, inline styling tags, and `<![CDATA[…]]>` sections are protected as
inline codes; and a comment immediately preceding an entry becomes a
translator note. Detection is content-based (a `<resources>` root with at
least one string/array/plurals element) so generic XML still routes to the
XML format. Output is a byte-faithful splice of the original — only changed
values are rewritten, preserving prolog, comments, whitespace, attribute
order, entities, and Android backslash escapes.

extractComments.description: Attach a preceding XML comment as a translator note.
extractComments.help:
When enabled (the default), an XML `<!-- comment -->` immediately
preceding an entry is attached to the block as a translator note.

skipNonTranslatable.help:
When enabled (the default), `<string>`/`<string-array>`/`<plurals>`
entries carrying `translatable="false"` are passed through untouched
and not extracted.

skipResourceReferences.description: Skip values that are bare resource references.
skipResourceReferences.help:
When enabled (the default), values that are a single resource reference
(`@string/foo`, `?attr/bar`) are not extracted as translatable text.

processingNotes: `<string>`, `<string-array>` items, and `<plurals>` items become translatable blocks; plural items are keyed by CLDR quantity.
Positional/printf args, `<xliff:g>`, inline styling, and CDATA are protected as inline codes; Android backslash escapes round-trip verbatim.
Detection is Sniff-based so plain XML documents are not claimed.

examples.title: Keep non-translatable entries in scope
examples.description: Extract even entries marked translatable="false".

entries.description: Extract and update translations in Apple String Catalog (.xcstrings) files used by Xcode 15+ projects.

schema.title: Apple String Catalog
schema.description: Configuration for the native Apple String Catalog (.xcstrings) reader/writer

extractStale.title: Extract stale entries
extractStale.description: Emit entries whose extractionState is "stale" (the source string no longer appears in the code base) as translatable blocks.

markTranslatedState.title: State for new translations
markTranslatedState.description: The stringUnit state value written for a localization populated for the first time. Existing states are preserved verbatim on round-trip.

doc.overview:
The **Apple String Catalog** format reads `.xcstrings` files — the default
localization container for iOS, macOS, and other Apple-platform projects
since Xcode 15. Unlike legacy `.strings`/`.stringsdict`, a single catalog
holds the source language and every translation in one JSON document,
carrying per-localization state (`new`, `translated`, `needs_review`),
developer comments, and plural / device / substitution variations.

Each translatable leaf value becomes a block. The source string (or the
entry key, when no source-language localization is present) is the source;
per-locale `stringUnit` values are the targets. Developer `comment`s surface
as translator notes, and `printf` specifiers (`%@`, `%lld`, `%1$@`, and
Apple's `%arg` substitution token) are protected as inline placeholders so
they are never altered. Writing back is byte-faithful: only changed values
and states are rewritten, leaving the rest of the catalog untouched.

extractStale.help:
When enabled (the default), entries marked `stale` by Xcode (the source
string no longer appears in the code base) are still emitted as
translatable blocks, since they remain localizable until removed.
Disable to skip stale entries.

markTranslatedState.description: State written when a previously untranslated locale is populated.
markTranslatedState.help:
The `state` value written into a localization's `stringUnit` when a
target translation is produced for a locale that had no entry. Apple
uses `translated`. Existing states are preserved verbatim on
round-trip; this only governs newly populated localizations.

processingNotes: A catalog is multilingual — one file carries the source language and all targets, so reading yields blocks with a source plus multiple target locales.
Plural (CLDR categories), device (iphone/ipad/mac/…), and substitution variations each expand into their own leaf blocks, keyed by the variation path.
printf specifiers and Apple's substitution `%arg` token are extracted as inline placeholders and must not be translated.
Output is byte-faithful: an unchanged catalog round-trips exactly; only modified values and states are rewritten.

examples.title: Skip stale entries
examples.description: Do not emit entries Xcode has flagged as stale.

examples.title: Mark new translations for review
examples.description: Write a needs_review state for newly populated localizations instead of translated.

entries.description: Extract and update translations in legacy Apple .strings and .stringsdict localization files.

schema.title: Apple Strings
schema.description: Configuration for the native Apple Strings (.strings + .stringsdict) reader/writer

extractComments.title: Extract comments as notes
extractComments.description: Surface a /* ... */ or // ... comment preceding a .strings entry as a translator note on the emitted block.

protectPlaceholders.title: Protect format placeholders
protectPlaceholders.description: Lift printf-style specifiers (%@, %lld, %1$@) and .stringsdict %#@var@ tokens into inline codes so they are never translated.

doc.overview:
The **Apple Strings** format reads both legacy Apple localization file
types in one format: the line-based `.strings` key/value tables and the
`.stringsdict` plist-XML plural dictionaries (locale by `.lproj` directory).

In `.strings`, a comment preceding an entry becomes a translator note and
printf specifiers (`%@`, `%lld`, `%1$@`) are protected as inline codes. In
`.stringsdict`, each `NSStringLocalizedFormatKey` and every CLDR plural
category (`zero`/`one`/`two`/`few`/`many`/`other`) is modeled as a block,
with `%#@var@` tokens protected. Writes are byte-faithful — only changed
values are spliced back — and UTF-8 and UTF-16 (LE/BE) encodings are
preserved. The modern `.xcstrings` String Catalog is handled by the
separate `xcstrings` format.

extractComments.description: Attach a preceding comment as a translator note (.strings).
extractComments.help:
When enabled (the default), a `/* … */` or `// …` comment preceding a
`.strings` entry is attached to the block as a translator note.

protectPlaceholders.description: Protect printf and %#@var@ tokens as inline codes.
protectPlaceholders.help:
When enabled (the default), printf specifiers (`%@`, `%lld`, `%1$@`)
and `.stringsdict` `%#@var@` substitution tokens are lifted into opaque
inline codes so they are never altered by translation.

processingNotes: `.strings` and `.stringsdict` are detected by their extensions; a stream without one is classified by content.
`.stringsdict` plural categories are modeled as separate blocks, like the String Catalog.
Binary-plist `.strings` is out of scope; UTF-8 and UTF-16 text encodings are preserved on round-trip.

examples.title: Leave placeholders inline
examples.description: Do not protect printf/%#@var@ tokens as codes.

inlineCodes.title: Inline Codes
inlineCodes.description: Pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, variables, tags) within translatable text.

enabled.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.)

rules.title: Code Finder Rules
rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text

rules.title: Patterns
rules.description: List of regex patterns to detect inline codes

pattern.title: Pattern
pattern.description: Regex pattern for inline code detection

sample.title: Sample Text
sample.description: Sample text to test patterns against

useAllRulesWhenTesting.title: Test All Rules Together
useAllRulesWhenTesting.description: Test all rules together or individually

mergeAdjacent.title: Merge Adjacent Codes
mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder

moveBoundaryCodes.title: Move Boundary Codes
moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable)

simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules
simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation

identification.title: File Identification

fileNames.title: File names
fileNames.description: Comma-delimited list of file names to be processed (wildcards are allowed) in the same order as configuration ids

mimeType.title: MIME type of the filter's container format

configIds.title: Filter configuration ids
configIds.description: Comma-delimited list of configuration ids corresponding to the file names

identification.description: Settings for identifying and matching files within the archive.

doc.overview: The Archive Filter processes files stored inside ZIP archive files (e.g. `.zip` or `.jar` files). It extracts files matching specified name patterns from the archive and processes each one using a designated Okapi filter configuration. This enables localization of translatable content embedded within archive formats.

identification.description: Settings for identifying and matching files within the archive.

identification.configIds.help:
Comma-separated list of Okapi filter configuration identifiers, one for each file name pattern.

- Must have **exactly as many entries** as file name patterns, in the **same order**
- Each config ID specifies which filter processes the files matched by the corresponding pattern
- Use default filter IDs (e.g. `okf_properties`, `okf_html`) for standard configurations, or custom configuration IDs for non-default settings
examples: `okf_properties` — process matched files with the Properties filter (default config)
`okf_tmx,okf_xliff,okf_xliff` — three filters matching three file name patterns

dependsOn.condition: must have the same number of comma-separated entries, paired by position

identification.fileNames.help:
Comma-separated list of file name patterns to match against entries **inside** the archive. Files matching any pattern will be extracted and processed with the corresponding filter.

- Patterns match file names in **any directory** within the archive
- Supports wildcard characters: `?` (single char) and `*` (any chars)
- The number of patterns must match the number of entries in **Filter configuration ids** — each pattern pairs with its corresponding config ID by position
identification.fileNames.values: Comma-delimited file name patterns. Wildcards `?` and `*` are supported.
examples: `Res.properties` — matches files named exactly `Res.properties` in any directory
`*.tmx,*.xlf,*.xlff` — matches all TMX and XLIFF files
`content/*.html,strings/*.properties` — matches HTML files in `content/` and properties files in `strings/`

identification.mimeType.help:
The MIME type identifying the **archive container** format itself, not the files inside it.

The default `application/x-archive` is appropriate for standard ZIP files. Only change this if you are processing a specialized archive format that requires a different MIME type.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Files inside the archive are matched by name pattern against entries in **any directory** within the ZIP structure.
Each file name pattern is paired positionally with a filter configuration ID — the first pattern uses the first config ID, the second pattern uses the second config ID, etc.

examples.title: Extract properties files from a JAR
examples.description: Process all `.properties` files inside a JAR archive using the Properties filter.

examples.title: Multiple file types in a ZIP
examples.description: Extract and process both TMX and XLIFF files from a ZIP archive, each with its appropriate filter.

examples.title: Named file in archive
examples.description: Extract only files named exactly `Res.properties` from the archive.

entries.description: Extract and reinsert translatable cells from CSV files, with column-based selection and quoting control.

schema.title: CSV Format
schema.description: Configuration for the CSV/TSV format reader/writer

codeFinderRules.title: Code Finder Rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

columnNamesRow.title: Column Names Row
columnNamesRow.description: 1-based row number containing column names. 0 means auto-detect.

commentColumns.title: Comment Columns
commentColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) that contain comments or notes

hasHeader.title: Has Header Row
hasHeader.description: If true, the first row is treated as headers (non-translatable)

keyColumns.title: Key Columns
keyColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) that provide the block ID (source ID / record ID)

separator.title: Field Delimiter
separator.description: Field delimiter character (comma for CSV, tab for TSV)

textQualifier.title: Text Qualifier
textQualifier.description: Character used to quote field values containing delimiters or newlines

translatableColumns.title: Translatable Columns
translatableColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) to extract as translatable content. If empty, all columns are translatable.

trimValues.title: Trim Values
trimValues.description: If true, leading and trailing whitespace is removed from cell values

useCodeFinder.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection within cell values

valuesStartRow.title: Values Start Row
valuesStartRow.description: 1-based row number where data values begin. 0 means auto-detect.

doc.overview:
The **CSV** format reads comma-separated value files, extracts selected
columns as translatable blocks, and writes the translations back into the
same rows. It handles quoted fields containing delimiters or newlines and
optional header rows. The same implementation backs the tab-separated TSV
format with a tab delimiter.

By default the first row is treated as a header and every data column is
translatable. You can restrict extraction to specific columns, designate key
columns that supply each block's ID, mark comment columns whose values
become notes, and tune the delimiter, quote character, and row offsets.

codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within cells.
codeFinderRules.help:
An array of regular expressions; text matching any of them is treated
as an inline code rather than translatable words. Only used when
`useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these patterns to apply

columnNamesRow.help:
The 1-based row that holds the column names. `0` means auto-detect: row
1 is used when `hasHeader` is enabled.

commentColumns.description: 0-based indices of columns holding comments or notes.
commentColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose values are recorded as notes on the
row's translatable blocks rather than extracted as content.

hasHeader.description: Treat the first row as a non-translatable header.
hasHeader.help:
When enabled (the default), the first row is treated as column headers
and is not extracted. Disable it for files whose data begins on the
first row.

keyColumns.description: 0-based indices of columns providing the block ID.
keyColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose values supply each block's ID
(source/record identifier). When several are given, their values are
joined with `.`. When empty, row-based IDs are used.
keyColumns.values: Array of 0-based integers (default empty — row-based IDs)
examples: `[0]` — use the first column as the record ID

separator.help: The character separating fields — a comma for CSV, a tab for TSV.

textQualifier.description: Quote character for fields with delimiters or newlines.
textQualifier.help:
The character used to quote field values that contain the delimiter, a
newline, or the quote character itself.

translatableColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose cells are extracted as translatable
content. When empty, every column is treated as translatable.
translatableColumns.values: Array of 0-based integers (default empty — all columns)
examples: `[1, 2]` — extract the second and third columns

trimValues.description: Strip surrounding whitespace from cell values.
trimValues.help:
When enabled, leading and trailing whitespace is removed from each cell
value before extraction.

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline-code detection.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes within cell values. The
patterns are supplied via `codeFinderRules`.

valuesStartRow.help:
The 1-based row from which data values are read. `0` means auto-detect:
the row after the header, or row 1 when there is no header.

limitations: Column selection is index-based; columns are not addressed by header name.
processingNotes: Each selected cell becomes a translatable block; row structure, delimiters, and quoting form the non-translatable skeleton.
Key columns supply IDs and comment columns supply notes; they are not themselves extracted as translatable content.

examples.title: Translate one column, keyed by another
examples.description: Extract column 2, using column 0 as the record ID.

examples.title: Headerless semicolon-delimited file
examples.description: Read a file with no header row and a semicolon delimiter.

inlineCodes.title: Inline codes
inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling

enabled.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.)

rules.title: Code Finder Rules
rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text

rules.title: Patterns
rules.description: List of regex patterns to detect inline codes

pattern.title: Pattern
pattern.description: Regex pattern for inline code detection

sample.title: Sample Text
sample.description: Sample text to test patterns against

useAllRulesWhenTesting.title: Test All Rules Together
useAllRulesWhenTesting.description: Test all rules together or individually

mergeAdjacent.title: Merge Adjacent Codes
mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder

moveBoundaryCodes.title: Move Boundary Codes
moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable)

simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules
simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation

columnNamesLineNum.title: Line with column names (0 if none)

numColumns.title: Number of Columns
numColumns.description: Fixed number of columns when column detection mode is set to fixed.

trimMode.title: Exclude leading/trailing white spaces from extracted text

doc.overview: The Comma-Separated Values Filter extracts translatable text from CSV-style files, where each record is a line and fields are separated by a single delimiter character (a comma by default, but configurable to semicolon, pipe, or any other character). Field values may be wrapped in a text qualifier so that the delimiter, line breaks, and the qualifier itself can appear inside a value. The filter handles multi-line quoted fields, two qualifier-escaping conventions, and optional headers, and shares the table family's column-role model for selecting source, target, ID, and comment columns. The `format.*` parameters below are specific to CSV; the column, header, and whitespace settings are inherited from the table filter.

codeFinderRules.help: The inline-code detection rules applied to extracted cell text, in Okapi `#v1` rule format. The defaults match printf-style format specifiers and common backslash escape sequences. Only applied when `useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these rules to apply

columnNamesLineNum.help: The 1-based line number holding the column captions. For CSV the names are read by splitting that line on the delimiter and stripping qualifiers; they label comment metadata and may set the pipeline locale when a column name is a locale code. Use `0` when the file has no column-name line.
notes: Lines are numbered from 1. For a CSV with a header on line 1 and data from line 2, set `columnNamesLineNum=1` and `valuesStartLineNum=2`.

commentColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of columns whose values attach as notes (or metadata, see `exposeCommentsAsMetadata`) to the source column they annotate via `commentSourceRefs`, rather than being extracted as translatable text.

dependsOn.condition: maps each comment column to the source column it annotates

commentSourceRefs.help: Maps each column in `commentColumns` to the source column it annotates, as a comma-separated list of 1-based source-column indices aligned position-by-position. The comment becomes a read-only note on that source text unit.

detectColumnsMode.help: How the column count per row is decided: per-row, from the number of names on the column-names line, or fixed. CSV rows already split cleanly on the delimiter, so this mainly matters when you need a guaranteed column count.

dependsOn.condition: used only when set to 2 (fixed number)
used only when set to 1 (from column names)

exposeCommentsAsMetadata.help: When enabled, comment-column values attach to the source text unit as metadata keyed by column name instead of as translator notes.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when comment columns are defined

format.addQualifiers.help: When enabled, the writer adds text qualifiers around output values that require them — values containing the delimiter, line breaks, or the qualifier — even if the corresponding source field was not qualified. Use it to guarantee well-formed CSV when translations introduce delimiters or newlines.

format.escapingMode.help: How a literal text qualifier embedded in a field value is escaped in the source. Duplication treats two consecutive qualifiers as one literal qualifier (the common CSV convention, e.g. `""`). Backslash treats a backslash before the qualifier as the escape (e.g. `\"`). This setting governs both how qualified fields are detected and how escaped qualifiers are recognised and preserved.

format.fieldDelimiter.help: The character that separates fields within a row. Any single character may be used — comma for CSV, semicolon and pipe being common alternatives. When empty, the line is not split and the whole line is treated as a single cell.
examples: `;` — semicolon-delimited
`|` — pipe-delimited

format.removeQualifiers.help: When enabled (the default), the surrounding text qualifiers are stripped from extracted values and kept in the skeleton, so translators see only the field content. When disabled, the qualifiers remain part of the extracted text.

format.textQualifier.help: The character placed before and after a field value so the value may contain the field delimiter, line breaks, or the qualifier itself. Qualified content is detected during parsing; whether the qualifier characters are kept in the extracted text depends on `format.removeQualifiers`.

mergeAdjacentCodes.help: When multiple inline codes appear consecutively with no text between them, they are combined into a single placeholder code. This simplifies the translatable content by reducing the number of inline codes translators must handle. For example, `` would become a single placeholder rather than two separate codes.

moveLeadingAndTrailingCodesToSkeleton.help: Inline codes appearing at the very beginning or end of a segment are moved into the skeleton (non-translatable portion). This reduces clutter in the translatable text when boundary codes carry no translational significance. For example, if a segment starts with `` and ends with ``, those codes are moved to skeleton so translators see only the text content.

numColumns.help: The fixed number of columns expected per row. Only consulted when `detectColumnsMode` is set to fixed-number. Rows with fewer cells are padded with empty cells; rows with more cells have the extras dropped.

dependsOn.condition: only active when detectColumnsMode = 2 (fixed number)

preserveWS.help: Inherited from the plain-text base. When enabled, whitespace inside extracted values is preserved; cell-edge trimming is governed by `trimMode`, `trimLeading`, and `trimTrailing`.

recordIdColumn.help: The 1-based index of the column whose value identifies the row. Combined with `sourceIdSuffixes` to synthesise an ID for source columns lacking an explicit ID cell.

sendColumnsMode.help: Which columns of each data row are extracted: none, only the columns named in the column-role settings, or all columns as source. `listed` mode enables bilingual extraction, IDs, and notes.
notes: Bilingual extraction (source paired with target) is only available in `listed` mode with a non-empty `targetColumns`.

sendHeaderMode.help: Which header-area lines are extracted as translatable text: none, the column-names line only, or all header lines.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation in extracted segments
simplifierRules.help:
Define rules for simplifying inline code representation in extracted text. Uses the Okapi simplifier rules syntax to merge or reduce inline codes into simpler placeholders.

This is edited via a dedicated rules editor widget.

sourceColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of the columns extracted as source (translatable) text. Consulted when `sendColumnsMode` is `listed`.
examples: `1,3` — extract columns 1 and 3 as source text

sourceIdColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of columns whose values become the resource names (IDs) of source text units, linked to their source column via `sourceIdSourceRefs`. An empty ID cell falls back to `recordIdColumn` + `sourceIdSuffixes`.

dependsOn.condition: maps each ID column to the source column it names

sourceIdSourceRefs.help: Maps each column in `sourceIdColumns` to the source column it names, as a comma-separated list of 1-based source-column indices aligned position-by-position.

sourceIdSuffixes.help: Suffixes appended to the `recordIdColumn` value to build an ID for a source column that has no explicit ID cell. Aligned position-by-position with `sourceColumns`.

targetColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of columns holding existing target text. Each is tied to a source column via `targetSourceRefs` and a locale via `targetLanguages`; supplying them makes the configuration bilingual.

dependsOn.condition: maps each target column to its source column
assigns the locale of each target column

targetLanguages.help: Locale of each target column, aligned position-by-position with `targetColumns`. A single value applies to all target columns; otherwise the pipeline target locale is used.
examples: `fr,de` — first target column is French, second is German

targetSourceRefs.help: Maps each column in `targetColumns` to the source column it translates, as a comma-separated list of 1-based source-column indices aligned position-by-position. A target whose reference cannot be resolved is sent to the skeleton.

trimLeading.help: Removes leading spaces and tabs from each extracted cell value. Forced on whenever `trimMode` is not none.

trimMode.help: How leading/trailing whitespace is removed from cells: none, non-qualified fields only (leaving the inside of quoted fields intact), or all fields. Any non-none value forces `trimLeading` and `trimTrailing` on. The non-qualified option is meaningful for CSV because it distinguishes quoted from unquoted fields.
notes: `non-qualified only` matters only for formats that have a text qualifier (CSV); for TSV and fixed-width it behaves like trimming all cells.

trimTrailing.help: Removes trailing spaces and tabs from each extracted cell value. Forced on whenever `trimMode` is not none.

unescapeSource.help: Inherited from the plain-text base. When enabled, `\t`, `\n`, `\\`, and `\uXXXX` escape sequences in extracted values are converted to the characters they represent.

useCodeFinder.help: Inherited from the plain-text base filter. When enabled, the regular expressions in `codeFinderRules` are applied to extracted cell text and matching runs are turned into inline codes rather than translatable words. Used to protect printf-style variables, escape sequences, and similar markup embedded in cell values.

dependsOn.condition: supplies the patterns applied when enabled

valuesStartLineNum.help: The 1-based line where data rows begin. Lines before it form the header area, handled per `sendHeaderMode`. For a header on line 1, set this to 2.

wrapMode.help: How a value spanning multiple physical lines (a quoted multi-line field) is represented: `NONE` joins wrapped lines with a line feed, `SPACES` joins with a single space, `PLACEHOLDERS` inserts an inline placeholder at each internal break so the wrapping can be reproduced on output.

limitations: Column roles are addressed by 1-based index, not by header name.
processingNotes: Fields are split on the single `format.fieldDelimiter` character; an empty delimiter falls back to treating the whole line as one cell.
A qualified value whose opening qualifier is not closed on the same line triggers multi-line merging: subsequent lines are folded into the field until the qualifier balances.
Embedded line breaks inside a quoted field are reproduced according to `wrapMode`; line breaks between records are preserved.
Two adjacent qualifiers inside a value (or a backslash-escaped qualifier) are moved to the skeleton as an inline code, so the literal qualifier survives the round-trip.

examples.title: CSV with a header row
examples.description: Standard CSV: column names on line 1, data from line 2, comma delimiter, double-quote qualifier. By default columns are extracted as listed (source column 1, target column 2).
examples.config:
```yaml
configId: okf_table_csv
format:
  fieldDelimiter: ","
  textQualifier: '"'
columnNamesLineNum: 1
valuesStartLineNum: 2
sendHeaderMode: 1
```
examples.input:
```csv
Name,Description
"Widget","A small, useful device"
```

examples.title: Semicolon-delimited, no header
examples.description: Read a file whose fields are separated by semicolons and whose data starts on the first line.

examples.title: Qualifier with embedded delimiter and escaped quote
examples.description: The text qualifier lets a field hold the delimiter; a literal qualifier inside the value is escaped by duplication (`""`) under the default escaping mode.
examples.input:
```csv
Name,Description
"Quote Test","She said ""hello"""
```

entries.description: Extract and update human-readable $description documentation in W3C DTCG design token files.

schema.title: Design Tokens (DTCG)
schema.description: Configuration for the W3C Design Tokens Community Group (DTCG) token format. Only $description documentation is translatable; token values and structure pass through untouched.

extractDescriptions.title: Extract $description documentation
extractDescriptions.description: Extract $description values (human-readable token and group documentation) as translatable blocks. This is the only natural-language field in DTCG; $value is always a typed design value (colour, dimension, font name, …) and is never extracted. Disable to read the file as fully non-translatable structure.

doc.overview:
The **Design Tokens (DTCG)** format reads W3C Design Tokens Community Group
token files (`.tokens` / `.tokens.json`) — the JSON interchange format used
by Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio, and similar tooling.

Token values are typed design data (colors, dimensions, font families,
aliases), not natural language, so the format extracts only the
human-readable `$description` documentation on tokens and groups as
translatable content; every token `$value`, `$type`, `$extensions`, and the
surrounding structure passes through untouched. Detection claims the unique
`.tokens` extension and sniffs DTCG content (`$value` + `$type`); `.tokens.json`
files resolve to the JSON format by extension unless `-f designtokens` is
given. Round-trip is byte-faithful, inherited from the JSON reader/writer.

extractDescriptions.help:
When enabled (the default), `$description` fields on tokens and groups
are extracted as translatable blocks. When disabled, the file yields no
translatable content.

processingNotes: Only `$description` is extracted; token values and structure are non-translatable passthrough.
A token file with no `$description` yields zero translatable blocks — expected for machine-generated token sets.

examples.title: Disable description extraction
examples.description: Treat the file as fully non-translatable.

entries.description: Extract translatable prose from Doxygen and Javadoc documentation comments in source code.

schema.title: Doxygen Comments
schema.description: Extracts translatable text from Doxygen/Javadoc comments in source code

codeFinderRules.title: Code finder rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

preserveWhitespace.title: Preserve whitespace
preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in extracted comment text instead of normalizing it

useCodeFinder.title: Enable inline code detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based detection of inline codes within translatable text

doc.overview:
The Doxygen format extracts the documentation prose from API comments
embedded in C, C++, Objective-C, Java, and Python source files. It
recognises the standard Doxygen comment styles — `///`, `//!`,
`/** */`, `/*! */`, and the trailing `///<` and `/*!< */` variants —
and leaves the surrounding code, command markers, and code or verbatim
blocks as non-translatable skeleton, so the file round-trips after
merge.

It understands the Doxygen command vocabulary: metadata commands such
as `\class` or `\file` are skipped, while description commands such as
`\brief`, `\param`, and `\return` contribute their description text as
translatable blocks. This mirrors Okapi's Doxygen filter and lets you
translate inline documentation without touching the code it documents.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in comment text.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within a translatable
block is protected as an inline code rather than translated. Used to
shield format specifiers or markup embedded in comments. Only takes
effect when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.
examples: `%[ds]` — protect `printf`-style format specifiers
`\\ref [A-Za-z_]+` — protect Doxygen cross-references

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

preserveWhitespace.description: Keep the original whitespace inside extracted comment text.
preserveWhitespace.help:
When disabled (the default), extracted comment text is trimmed and
its internal whitespace is normalised so translators see clean
prose. Enable it for code samples or pre-formatted paragraphs inside
comments where indentation and spacing are meaningful and must be
preserved verbatim.

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside comment text using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes (placeholders,
variables, tags) within the extracted comment text. The patterns are
supplied in `codeFinderRules`. When disabled (the default), no
pattern-based inline codes are extracted.

processingNotes: Metadata commands (e.g. `\class`, `\file`) are skipped; description commands (e.g. `\brief`, `\param`, `\return`) extract their description text.
Code and verbatim blocks within comments are preserved as non-translatable skeleton.

examples.title: Preserve comment whitespace
examples.description: Keep indentation and spacing in pre-formatted comment text.

examples.title: Protect format specifiers
examples.description: Treat printf-style specifiers as inline codes instead of translating them.

inlineCodes.title: Inline Codes
inlineCodes.description: Pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, variables, tags) within translatable text.

enabled.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.)

rules.title: Code Finder Rules
rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text

rules.title: Patterns
rules.description: List of regex patterns to detect inline codes

pattern.title: Pattern
pattern.description: Regex pattern for inline code detection

sample.title: Sample Text
sample.description: Sample text to test patterns against

useAllRulesWhenTesting.title: Test All Rules Together
useAllRulesWhenTesting.description: Test all rules together or individually

mergeAdjacent.title: Merge Adjacent Codes
mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder

moveBoundaryCodes.title: Move Boundary Codes
moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable)

simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules
simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation

path.title: Comment Style Path
path.description: Path pattern defining which Doxygen comment style to extract (C++, Javadoc, Qt, or Python).

doc.overview: The Doxygen Filter extracts [Doxygen](http://www.stack.nl/~dimitri/doxygen/)-style comments from source code for translation. It supports C++-style (`///`), Javadoc-style (`/**`), Qt-style (`/*!`), and Python-style (`'''` or `"""`) comment blocks. Doxygen special commands, HTML commands, and XML commands are recognized and converted to inline codes. The filter preserves line numbers so that source and translated line numbers maintain a one-to-one correspondence.

parser.doxygenCommands.help:
Defines how known Doxygen special commands are handled during extraction. Pre-populated with the full set of [Doxygen special commands](http://www.stack.nl/~dimitri/doxygen/commands.html). Authored under the Okapi config key `doxygen_commands`.

Each entry is keyed by command name (without prefix/suffix, case-sensitive) and supports:
- `type` — `PLACEHOLDER`, `OPENING`, or `CLOSING`
- `inline` — `true` for inline, `false` for block-level (default: `false`)
- `pair` — paired command name (e.g., `code` pairs with `endcode`)
- `translatable` — whether the block content is translatable (default: `true`)
- `parameters` — ordered list of parameter descriptors, each with:
  - `name` — organizational label (not used by filter)
  - `length` — `WORD`, `LINE`, `PHRASE`, or `PARAGRAPH`
  - `required` — affects how aggressively text is parsed as a parameter (default: `true`)
  - `translatable` — whether the parameter is translatable (default: `true`)
parser.doxygenCommands.values: Each command entry is keyed by the command name as it appears in the Doxygen comment (e.g., `code` for `\code`). `type` must be one of `PLACEHOLDER`, `OPENING`, or `CLOSING`. Parameter `length` must be one of `WORD`, `LINE`, `PHRASE` (double-quote delimited string), or `PARAGRAPH`.
notes: The `parameters` listing is optional. When present, parameters must be listed in the order they appear after the command.
Parameters with non-whitespace delimiters (e.g. `.py` in `\code{.py}`) are **not** currently supported.
examples:
```yaml
doxygen_commands:
  code:
    type: OPENING
    inline: false
    pair: endcode
    translatable: false
```

```yaml
doxygen_commands:
  brief:
    type: PLACEHOLDER
    inline: true
    parameters:
      - name: description
        length: LINE
        required: true
        translatable: true
```

parser.htmlCommands.help: Defines how HTML commands within Doxygen comments are handled. Pre-populated with the full set of [Doxygen HTML commands](http://www.stack.nl/~dimitri/doxygen/htmlcmds.html). Uses the same entry format as `doxygenCommands`. Authored under the Okapi config key `html_commands`. Resolved by command name with the leading `<` retained, so opening and closing HTML tags are matched against this map.

parser.preserveWhitespace.help: Prevents the filter from collapsing whitespace in extracted comment text. By default (`false`), the filter collapses single linebreaks within a text run, producing potentially very long single-line paragraphs. No effort is made to enforce a maximum line width on output. Authored under the Okapi config key `preserve_whitespace`.
notes: Without this option, each translatable paragraph is collapsed to a single (potentially very long) line.

limitations: Single linebreaks in a text run that are not part of a Doxygen command are collapsed into a single long line. No maximum line width is enforced on output.
Command parameters with non-whitespace delimiters (e.g. `.py` in `\code{.py}`) are not supported.
Non-translatable command parameters are not exposed for any special processing.
processingNotes: **Encoding detection**: Uses Unicode BOM if present (UTF-8, UTF-16, etc.); otherwise falls back to the default encoding specified when opening the document.
**Inline codes**: The full set of Doxygen special commands, HTML commands, and XML commands are recognized and converted to inline codes in extracted text units.
**Line number preservation**: The filter maintains a one-to-one correspondence between source and translated line numbers.

examples.title: Extracting Doxygen comments
examples.description: Shows how various Doxygen comment styles are recognized and extracted for translation.

examples.title: Inline code conversion from commands
examples.description: Doxygen commands like `\class` and `\brief` are converted to inline codes, while translatable text is preserved as text units.
examples.input:
```cpp
/*! \class Test class.h "inc/class.h"
 *  \brief This is a test class.
 *
 * Some details about the Test class
 */
```
examples.output:
```
Text Unit 1: <1/><2/> This is a test class.
Text Unit 2: Some details about the Test class
```

examples.title: Custom command with regex pattern
examples.description: Defines a custom command using a regex pattern to match application-specific markers in comments.
examples.config:
```yaml
custom_commands:
  - pattern: "###ACCESS_CHECKS###.*?;"
    type: PLACEHOLDER
    inline: true
```

entries.description: Extract translatable text from entity declarations in XML DTD files.

doc.overview:
A DTD (Document Type Definition) declares the structure and entities of an
XML vocabulary. This reader targets DTDs used for user-interface
localization — most notably the Mozilla DTD files referenced by XUL
documents — where translatable strings are stored as general entity
declarations.

neokapi reads and writes DTD files. Each general (non-parameter)
`<!ENTITY>` declaration becomes one translatable Block whose source is the
decoded entity value and whose name is the entity identifier. A comment
immediately preceding an entity rides along as a note annotation on that
Block. Everything else — `<!ELEMENT>` and `<!ATTLIST>` declarations,
processing instructions, parameter entities, and external entities — is
preserved as skeleton and is not extracted. Standard XML entities and
numeric character references inside values are decoded on read and
re-encoded on write. This mirrors the upstream Okapi DTD filter; the native
reader exposes no configurable JSON schema in this reference.
limitations: Only general entity declarations are translatable; element, attribute-list, and parameter/external entity declarations are skeleton-only.
Inline-code (code finder) handling that the upstream filter exposes is not surfaced as a configurable schema property in this reference.
processingNotes: One translatable Block per general `<!ENTITY>` declaration; the entity name becomes the Block name.
A comment directly preceding an entity is attached to that Block as a note.
Standard XML entities and numeric character references in values are decoded on read and re-encoded on write.

useCodeFinder.title: Use Code Finder
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regular expression-based inline code detection in extracted text.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation
simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules

doc.overview: This filter processes DTD (Document Type Definition) documents containing translatable text entity declarations. It is primarily intended for XML-DTD files like Mozilla DTD files used with XUL documents for user-interface localization. The filter extracts text from `<!ENTITY>` declarations for translation.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular expressions in #v1 format defining patterns to treat as inline codes.
codeFinderRules.help:
Regular expression rules in Okapi `#v1` format that define patterns to match as inline codes within extracted text.

The format starts with `#v1`, followed by `count.i=N` (number of rules), then `rule0=...`, `rule1=...`, etc.

**Important:** When writing rules in YAML configuration, backslashes must be double-escaped (e.g., `\\b` for a word boundary `\b`).
codeFinderRules.values:
Okapi `#v1` code finder format:
```
#v1\ncount.i=<number_of_rules>\nrule0=<regex>\nrule1=<regex>...
```
Each rule is a Java regular expression.
notes: Backslashes in regular expressions must be escaped for YAML notation — use `\\b` to represent `\b` in the actual regex.
examples: `#v1\ncount.i=1\nrule0=\\bVAR\\d\\b` — matches variable placeholders like `VAR1`, `VAR2`

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regular expression-based inline code detection in extracted text.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables the inline code finder, which uses regular expressions to identify spans of extracted text that should be treated as inline codes rather than translatable content.

Useful when entity values contain variables or placeholders (e.g. `VAR1`) that must be protected from modification during translation.

processingNotes: Input encoding is determined by BOM if present; otherwise falls back to the default encoding specified in filter options.
UTF-8 output includes a BOM only if the input was UTF-8 and had a BOM; otherwise no BOM is written.
Output line-break style matches the original input document.

examples.title: Mozilla DTD with variables
examples.description: Extracting translatable entity values from a Mozilla-style DTD file, with variable placeholders protected as inline codes using the code finder.
examples.input:
```xml
<!--Comments-->
<!ENTITY findWindow.title "Find Files">
<!ENTITY fileMenu.label "File">
<!ENTITY dialog.fileCount "Number of files = VAR1">
```

entries.description: Extract translatable text from EPUB e-books by walking the reading-order spine.

doc.overview:
EPUB is the open e-book format: a ZIP container (OCF) packaging XHTML and
SVG content documents alongside a Package Document (OPF) that lists the
manifest and the reading-order spine. neokapi reads the
`META-INF/container.xml` entry to locate the OPF, parses the OPF to resolve
the spine, and walks each spine item as a child layer of the document.

neokapi reads and writes EPUB, round-tripping the container so the
translated output remains a valid e-book. Content documents are parsed as
embedded XHTML: when an HTML sub-format reader is wired, each spine item is
delegated to it; otherwise neokapi falls back to a direct XHTML walk that
treats common block elements (paragraphs, headings, list items, table cells,
figure and table captions, blockquotes, and the document title) as
translatable. The OPF, container metadata, and non-content resources are
preserved as skeleton. This format has no configurable JSON schema in this
reference.
limitations: The fallback XHTML walk extracts a fixed set of block elements; richer extraction depends on an HTML sub-format reader being wired into the pipeline.
Spine items not listed in the OPF reading order are not walked for translatable text.
processingNotes: The OPF is located via `META-INF/container.xml`; spine items are walked in reading order, each as a child layer.
Content documents are processed as embedded XHTML, either through an HTML sub-format reader or a direct token walk.
The container structure, OPF, and non-content resources are preserved as skeleton for round-trips.

entries.description: Run an external subprocess as a custom extractor that streams blocks as NDJSON.

doc.overview:
The exec format is a declarative wrapper around an external
extractor. Instead of parsing a file format itself, it launches a
subprocess you name, feeds it the matched file paths on standard
input (NUL-separated), and reads back translatable blocks as
newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) on standard output. Each output line
is a record of the form `{"type":"block","document":"<path>",
"block":{ … }}`; lines that are not JSON objects are treated as noise
(progress or log output) and ignored. A non-zero exit status surfaces
as an error with the subprocess's captured stderr attached.

Use exec when a format has no built-in reader but you can extract its
strings with a script or tool in any language — for example a
project-specific source-code extractor. The protocol is transparent
to inspect (plain JSON) and carries no kapi-specific schema beyond the
block shape the subprocess emits.

The exec format is opt-in per project: it has no file extensions or
MIME types and is never auto-detected by file type. It is declared on
a collection's `format:` block in a `.kapi` project and run via
`kapi extract -p project.kapi`, which spawns the declared command once
per collection with every matched path on stdin. It is not usable as a
per-file reader; opening a file directly with this format returns an
error pointing at the project-driven path.
limitations: Not usable as a standalone per-file reader; it only runs through the project extraction path (`kapi extract -p`).
The subprocess must implement the NUL-separated-paths-in, NDJSON-blocks-out protocol; no other transport is supported.
There is no built-in writer; exec covers extraction only.
processingNotes: The subprocess receives matched file paths on stdin as a NUL-separated list and emits one `block` record per translatable unit on stdout.
The launch command, working directory, and an optional timeout are taken from the collection's format configuration in the `.kapi` project.
Output lines that are not JSON objects are skipped, so the subprocess may interleave progress text on stdout.

examples.title: Wrap a custom extractor
examples.description:
Declare an external command on a collection. `kapi extract` runs
it once per collection, passing matched paths on stdin and reading
NDJSON block records from stdout.
examples.config:
format:
  name: exec
  config:
    command: "vp kapi-react extract --stream"

entries.description: Read and write fixed-width column text files, extracting the columns you mark as translatable.

doc.overview:
Fixed-width column files are tabular text where every record shares
the same column layout: each column begins at a fixed character
offset and spans a fixed width, padded with whitespace to fill its
slot. Records are separated by line breaks.

The reader splits each line at the configured column boundaries and
emits one translatable block per non-empty cell in a column you have
marked translatable. Non-translatable cells are preserved as
structure so the writer can rebuild the original layout. There is no
auto-detection of the layout — you must declare the columns.

Configuration is supplied on the collection's `format:` block in a
`.kapi` project. The keys are: `columns` — an ordered list of
`{ name, start, width, translatable }` records, where `start` is the
0-based character offset, `width` is the column width in characters,
and `translatable` marks the column for extraction (at least one
column is required, each with a non-empty name, a positive width, and
a non-negative start); `hasHeader` — when true, treat the first row
as a header; and `trimValues` — when true, trim leading and trailing
whitespace from each cell's value.
limitations: The column layout must be declared explicitly; there is no auto-detection of column boundaries.
Offsets and widths are counted in characters (runes); multibyte characters occupy one position each, which may differ from byte-oriented layouts.
processingNotes: One block per non-empty translatable cell; non-translatable cells are emitted as Data so the layout round-trips.
With `hasHeader` enabled the first row is treated as a header rather than data.
Padding whitespace is preserved by default; set `trimValues` to strip it from extracted values.

examples.title: Extract a translatable column
examples.description:
A two-column layout where only the second column (the label) is
translatable; the first column is an identifier.
examples.config:
format:
  name: fixedwidth
  config:
    columns:
      - { name: id, start: 0, width: 8, translatable: false }
      - { name: label, start: 8, width: 40, translatable: true }
    trimValues: true

inlineCodes.description: Inline code detection and handling

enabled.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.)

rules.title: Code Finder Rules
rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text

rules.title: Patterns
rules.description: List of regex patterns to detect inline codes

pattern.title: Pattern
pattern.description: Regex pattern for inline code detection

sample.description: Sample text to test patterns against

useAllRulesWhenTesting.title: Test All Rules Together
useAllRulesWhenTesting.description: Test all rules together or individually

mergeAdjacent.title: Merge Adjacent Codes
mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder

moveBoundaryCodes.title: Move Boundary Codes
moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable)

simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules
simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation

columnNamesLineNum.title: Line with column names (0 if none)

numColumns.title: Number of Columns
numColumns.description: Fixed number of columns when column detection mode is set to fixed.

trimMode.title: Exclude leading/trailing white spaces from extracted text

doc.overview: The Fixed-Width Columns Filter extracts translatable text from tables in which each column occupies a fixed character span and is padded with whitespace, rather than being marked by a delimiter. Column boundaries are declared explicitly through paired start and end positions — there is no auto-detection of the layout. Each declared column is cut from the line by character position, the slice between its end and the next column's start is kept as skeleton padding so the layout round-trips, and the resulting cell is processed under the table family's column-role, header, and whitespace settings. By default it trims all cells to remove the alignment padding.

codeFinderRules.help: The inline-code detection rules applied to extracted cell text, in Okapi `#v1` rule format. The defaults match printf-style format specifiers and common backslash escape sequences. Only applied when `useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these rules to apply

columnNamesLineNum.help: The 1-based line number holding the column captions. The line is cut into cells by the declared column positions to read the names, which label comment metadata and may set the pipeline locale when a column name is a locale code. Use `0` when there is no column-name line.
notes: Lines are numbered from 1. For a CSV with a header on line 1 and data from line 2, set `columnNamesLineNum=1` and `valuesStartLineNum=2`.

columns.columnEndPositions.help: The 1-based character position at which each fixed-width column ends, as a comma-separated list aligned position-by-position with `columns.columnStartPositions`. The slice taken for a column runs from its start up to (but not including) its end; the gap between this end and the next column's start is preserved as skeleton padding.

dependsOn.condition: each end must be paired with a matching start position

columns.columnStartPositions.help: The 1-based character position at which each fixed-width column begins, as a comma-separated list aligned position-by-position with `columns.columnEndPositions`. A position counts characters from the start of the line. Only as many columns are read as the shorter of the start and end lists.

dependsOn.condition: each start must be paired with a matching end position

commentColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of columns whose values attach as notes (or metadata, see `exposeCommentsAsMetadata`) to the source column they annotate via `commentSourceRefs`, rather than being extracted as translatable text.

dependsOn.condition: maps each comment column to the source column it annotates

commentSourceRefs.help: Maps each column in `commentColumns` to the source column it annotates, as a comma-separated list of 1-based source-column indices aligned position-by-position. The comment becomes a read-only note on that source text unit.

detectColumnsMode.help: How the column count per row is decided. For fixed-width input the column count is effectively governed by the declared position lists; this setting is consulted in the same way as for other table formats.

dependsOn.condition: used only when set to 2 (fixed number)
used only when set to 1 (from column names)

exposeCommentsAsMetadata.help: When enabled, comment-column values attach to the source text unit as metadata keyed by column name instead of as translator notes.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when comment columns are defined

mergeAdjacentCodes.help: When multiple inline codes appear consecutively with no text between them, they are combined into a single placeholder code. This simplifies the translatable content by reducing the number of inline codes translators must handle. For example, `` would become a single placeholder rather than two separate codes.

moveLeadingAndTrailingCodesToSkeleton.help: Inline codes appearing at the very beginning or end of a segment are moved into the skeleton (non-translatable portion). This reduces clutter in the translatable text when boundary codes carry no translational significance. For example, if a segment starts with `` and ends with ``, those codes are moved to skeleton so translators see only the text content.

numColumns.help: The fixed number of columns expected per row. Only consulted when `detectColumnsMode` is set to fixed-number. Rows with fewer cells are padded with empty cells; rows with more cells have the extras dropped.

dependsOn.condition: only active when detectColumnsMode = 2 (fixed number)

preserveWS.help: Inherited from the plain-text base. When enabled, whitespace inside extracted values is preserved; cell-edge trimming is governed by `trimMode`, `trimLeading`, and `trimTrailing`. For fixed-width input the alignment padding is normally removed by the default trim mode.

recordIdColumn.help: The 1-based index of the column whose value identifies the row. Combined with `sourceIdSuffixes` to synthesise an ID for source columns lacking an explicit ID cell.

sendColumnsMode.help: Which columns of each data row are extracted: none, only the columns named in the column-role settings, or all columns as source. `listed` mode enables bilingual extraction, IDs, and notes.
notes: Bilingual extraction (source paired with target) is only available in `listed` mode with a non-empty `targetColumns`.

sendHeaderMode.help: Which header-area lines are extracted as translatable text: none, the column-names line only, or all header lines.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation in extracted segments
simplifierRules.help:
Define rules for simplifying inline code representation in extracted text. Uses the Okapi simplifier rules syntax to merge or reduce inline codes into simpler placeholders.

This is edited via a dedicated rules editor widget.

sourceColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of the columns extracted as source (translatable) text. The index refers to the column's position in the declared start/end lists. Consulted when `sendColumnsMode` is `listed`.
examples: `1,3` — extract columns 1 and 3 as source text

sourceIdColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of columns whose values become the resource names (IDs) of source text units, linked to their source column via `sourceIdSourceRefs`. An empty ID cell falls back to `recordIdColumn` + `sourceIdSuffixes`.

dependsOn.condition: maps each ID column to the source column it names

sourceIdSourceRefs.help: Maps each column in `sourceIdColumns` to the source column it names, as a comma-separated list of 1-based source-column indices aligned position-by-position.

sourceIdSuffixes.help: Suffixes appended to the `recordIdColumn` value to build an ID for a source column that has no explicit ID cell. Aligned position-by-position with `sourceColumns`.

targetColumns.help: Comma-separated 1-based indices of columns holding existing target text. Each is tied to a source column via `targetSourceRefs` and a locale via `targetLanguages`; supplying them makes the configuration bilingual.

dependsOn.condition: maps each target column to its source column
assigns the locale of each target column

targetLanguages.help: Locale of each target column, aligned position-by-position with `targetColumns`. A single value applies to all target columns; otherwise the pipeline target locale is used.
examples: `fr,de` — first target column is French, second is German

targetSourceRefs.help: Maps each column in `targetColumns` to the source column it translates, as a comma-separated list of 1-based source-column indices aligned position-by-position. A target whose reference cannot be resolved is sent to the skeleton.

trimLeading.help: Removes leading spaces and tabs from each extracted cell value. Forced on whenever `trimMode` is not none.

trimMode.help: How leading/trailing whitespace is removed from cells. For fixed-width columns the default is `all`, so the whitespace padding that aligns the columns is stripped from extracted values. Any non-none value forces `trimLeading` and `trimTrailing` on.
trimMode.values: `0` = none, `1` = non-qualified only, `2` = all (default `2` for fixed-width)
notes: `non-qualified only` matters only for formats that have a text qualifier (CSV); for TSV and fixed-width it behaves like trimming all cells.

trimTrailing.help: Removes trailing spaces and tabs from each extracted cell value. Forced on whenever `trimMode` is not none.

unescapeSource.help: Inherited from the plain-text base. When enabled, `\t`, `\n`, `\\`, and `\uXXXX` escape sequences in extracted values are converted to the characters they represent.

useCodeFinder.help: Inherited from the plain-text base filter. When enabled, the regular expressions in `codeFinderRules` are applied to extracted cell text and matching runs are turned into inline codes rather than translatable words. Used to protect printf-style variables, escape sequences, and similar markup embedded in cell values.

dependsOn.condition: supplies the patterns applied when enabled

valuesStartLineNum.help: The 1-based line where data rows begin. Lines before it form the header area, handled per `sendHeaderMode`.

wrapMode.help: How a value spanning multiple physical lines is represented: `NONE` joins wrapped lines with a line feed, `SPACES` joins with a single space, `PLACEHOLDERS` inserts an inline placeholder at each internal break so the wrapping can be reproduced on output.

limitations: The column layout must be declared explicitly via `columns.columnStartPositions` and `columns.columnEndPositions`; there is no auto-detection of boundaries.
Positions are counted in characters, 1-based; multibyte characters occupy one position each.
Column roles are addressed by 1-based index, not by header name.
processingNotes: Only as many columns are read as the shorter of the two position lists; a column whose start is not before its end, or whose start is past the line length, is skipped.
The text between a column's end position and the next column's start position is preserved as skeleton padding, so the fixed-width layout is reproduced on output.
Each cell records a `column_width` property alongside the usual line/column/row numbers.
The default trim mode is `all` so the surrounding alignment whitespace is stripped from extracted values.

examples.title: Two fixed-width columns
examples.description: An 8-character ID column followed by a 40-character label column. Column 1 spans positions 1-8 and column 2 spans 9-48; the label column is the source.
examples.config:
```yaml
configId: okf_table_fwc
columns:
  columnStartPositions: "1,9"
  columnEndPositions: "8,48"
sourceColumns: "2"
sendColumnsMode: 1
```
examples.input:
```
ID0001  Open the main valve
ID0002  Close the bypass
```

examples.title: Header line above fixed-width data
examples.description: Skip a header line and start the fixed-width records on line 2.

entries.description: Extract and update translatable messages in Flutter Application Resource Bundle (.arb) files.

schema.title: Flutter ARB
schema.description: Configuration for the native Flutter Application Resource Bundle (.arb) reader/writer

descriptionNotes.title: Surface descriptions as notes
descriptionNotes.description: Surface the "description" field of a resource's sibling "@<id>" attributes object as a translator note. The attributes object is always preserved byte-faithfully on round-trip regardless of this setting.

doc.overview:
The **Flutter ARB** format reads `.arb` files — the JSON localization
format used by Flutter's `gen-l10n` tooling and the Dart `intl` package.
Each file is monolingual: one locale (named by `@@locale`, or inferred from
the filename), where resource keys map to ICU MessageFormat message strings.

Each message becomes a translatable block. Sibling `@<id>` objects describe a
resource (its `description` and `placeholders`) and `@@<global>` keys hold
file metadata (`@@locale`, `@@last_modified`, `@@author`, `@@context`); both
are preserved verbatim and never extracted. ICU placeholders (`{name}`),
plurals (`{count, plural, …}`), and select/gender (`{g, select, …}`) are
protected as opaque inline codes so their syntax is never translated, and
`@description` is surfaced as a translator note. Output is byte-faithful:
key order, JSON escaping, and all `@`/`@@` metadata round-trip untouched.

descriptionNotes.description: Surface each resource's @description as a translator note.
descriptionNotes.help:
When enabled (the default), the `description` field of a message's
sibling `@<id>` attribute object is attached to the block as a
translator note. Disable to ignore descriptions.

processingNotes: One file = one locale; the message value is the block's source content.
`@<id>` attribute objects and `@@<global>` metadata are preserved but not extracted as translatable content.
ICU MessageFormat constructs (placeholders, plural, select, gender) are protected as inline codes, not translatable structure.

examples.title: Ignore descriptions
examples.description: Do not turn @description values into translator notes.

entries.description: Extract and reinsert translatable text from HTML, with element and attribute extraction rules.

schema.title: HTML Format
schema.description: Configuration for the native HTML format reader/writer

attributes.title: Attribute Rules
attributes.description: Global attribute extraction rules -- maps attribute names to their rule configuration (ruleTypes, allElementsExcept, onlyTheseElements, conditions)

codeFinderRules.title: Code finder rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

elements.title: Element Rules
elements.description: Element extraction rules -- maps element names to their rule configuration (ruleTypes, conditions, idAttributes, translatableAttributes)

parser.title: Parser behavior
parser.description: Settings that control how the HTML parser reads input

preserveWhitespace.title: Preserve whitespace
preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in extracted text instead of collapsing it

useCodeFinder.title: Enable inline code detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, variables, tags) within translatable text

doc.overview:
The **HTML** format reads HTML documents, extracts translatable text and
localizable attributes, and writes the translations back while preserving
the surrounding markup. Inline elements (such as `b`, `i`, `a`) become inline
codes within a block, so formatting and links survive translation.

The reader ships with sensible defaults for which elements hold translatable
text, which are inline, and which attributes (such as `alt` and `title`) are
localizable. The `elements` and `attributes` maps let you override or extend
those rules per element and per attribute, mirroring the okf_html bridge
configuration. Parser behaviour (whitespace handling) is grouped under
`parser`.

attributes.help:
A map from attribute name to a rule object. A rule may carry `ruleTypes`
(`ATTRIBUTE_TRANS`, `ATTRIBUTE_ID`, `ATTRIBUTE_WRITABLE`,
`ATTRIBUTE_READONLY`), an element scope via `onlyTheseElements` or
`allElementsExcept`, and a `conditions` triplet.
attributes.values: Map of attribute name → rule object (default: built-in rules)
examples: `{ alt: { ruleTypes: [ATTRIBUTE_TRANS] } }` — extract all `alt` values

codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within text.
codeFinderRules.help:
An array of regular expressions; text matching any of them is treated
as an inline code rather than translatable words. Only used when
`useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these patterns to apply

elements.help:
A map from element name to a rule object that controls how the element
is treated. A rule may carry `ruleTypes` (`TEXTUNIT`, `INLINE`,
`EXCLUDE`, `INCLUDE`, `ATTRIBUTES_ONLY`, `PRESERVE_WHITESPACE`, `GROUP`),
a `conditions` triplet, `idAttributes`, and a `translatableAttributes`
mapping. These override or extend the built-in element handling.
elements.values: Map of element name → rule object (default: built-in rules)
examples: `{ summary: { ruleTypes: [TEXTUNIT] } }` — extract `summary` element text

parser.preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve whitespace in extracted text instead of collapsing it.
parser.preserveWhitespace.help:
When enabled, original whitespace in text content is preserved. By
default whitespace is collapsed, matching how browsers render HTML.
Grouped under the `parser` object in the config.

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline-code detection.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, variables,
tags) within translatable text, beyond the inline HTML elements already
recognized. The patterns are supplied via `codeFinderRules`.

limitations: The reader applies built-in element/attribute defaults; the `elements` and `attributes` maps adjust them rather than replacing the entire rule set.
processingNotes: Inline elements become inline codes within blocks; block-level elements form the surrounding structure.
Localizable attributes (such as `alt` and `title`) are extracted as their own translatable units.
