doc.overview: The PDF Filter extracts text content from PDF files for processing. It does not handle complex formatting like tables or multi-level lists. The typical use case is scraping text from PDFs for quick word counts and leverage analysis. **Important:** This filter does not merge back into PDF format — it produces plain text output upon merging.

output.lineSeparator.help: The string written between consecutive lines of extracted text. Passed straight to Apache PDFBox `PDFTextStripper.setLineSeparator`. Change it to control how wrapped lines within a paragraph are joined in the plain-text output.
output.lineSeparator.values: String. Default is a single line feed (`\n`).

output.paragraphSeparator.help: The string written at the end of each detected paragraph. Passed to Apache PDFBox `PDFTextStripper.setParagraphEnd`. Set it to a double line feed to make paragraph breaks visible in the output, distinct from line breaks within a paragraph.
output.paragraphSeparator.values: String. Default is a single line feed (`\n`).

parser.indentThreshold.help: Minimum left-indent, measured in multiples of the space-character width, that PDFBox's layout analysis requires before it treats a line as the start of a new paragraph. Passed to `PDFTextStripper.setIndentThreshold` after parsing to a float. Lower it for documents with shallow indents that should still break paragraphs; raise it to merge slightly-indented lines into the preceding paragraph.
parser.indentThreshold.values: Decimal number as a string (parsed as a Java `float`). Default `"2.0"`.

parser.spacingTolerance.help: Spacing tolerance, as a fraction of the space-character width, that PDFBox uses to decide whether the gap between two glyphs is a word boundary (white space). Passed to `PDFTextStripper.setSpacingTolerance` after parsing to a float. Decrease it when glyphs are set close together and words run into each other; increase it when single words are split apart by spurious spaces.
parser.spacingTolerance.values: Decimal number as a string (parsed as a Java `float`). Default `"0.5"`.

limitations: This filter merges back in **plain text format**, not PDF.
Does not handle complex formatting like tables or multi-level lists.
Any encoding selected in tools like Rainbow will be ignored — the filter forces UTF-16.
processingNotes: PDF files are binary and do not have a specific encoding. Okapi extracts all text as a Java string and forces the encoding to `UTF-16`.
TextUnits are created following the default rules of the Plain Text filter — any text followed by a newline creates a new TextUnit (paragraph).
Extraction is performed by Apache PDFBox's `PDFTextStripper`. The filter exposes the stripper's layout-detection thresholds and the separators it writes between lines and paragraphs.

examples.title: Tune paragraph detection for tightly-laid-out PDFs
examples.description: Lower the indent threshold so smaller indents still start a new paragraph, and tighten the spacing tolerance so closely-set glyphs are not joined into one word. Use this when the default heuristics merge separate paragraphs or run words together.

examples.title: Use blank lines between paragraphs
examples.description: Emit an extra newline at the end of each detected paragraph so paragraph boundaries are visible in the extracted plain text, while keeping single newlines between wrapped lines.

entries.description: Scrape text content from PDF files for word counts and leverage analysis.

doc.overview:
PDF (Portable Document Format) is a page-description format. neokapi's PDF
reader is a content-stream walker: it locates the text-showing operators in
each page's content streams (inflating FlateDecode segments first) and
collects the literal and hex strings they draw. The literal-string scanner
honours the PDF string grammar, including escape sequences and balanced
nested parentheses. Every text run inside a page becomes one Block joined
with single spaces; there is no paragraph segmentation.

PDF support is intended for scraping text — word counts, leverage analysis,
and quick content review — not for layout-preserving round-trips. The reader
does not consult font dictionaries, so strings encoded as glyph indices
through a ToUnicode CMap (some CID/Type0 fonts) are filtered out rather than
surfaced as garbage. The **writer is degraded**: merging produces plain text
output, not a reconstructed PDF. This format has no configurable JSON schema
in this reference.
limitations: Output is plain text, not PDF — there is no layout-preserving round-trip.
Complex structure such as tables and multi-level lists is not reconstructed; there is no paragraph segmentation.
Text encoded as glyph indices through a ToUnicode CMap (some CID/Type0 fonts) is dropped rather than surfaced.
processingNotes: Reads text from content streams via the text-showing operators (`Tj`, `TJ`, `'`, `"`), inflating FlateDecode segments.
Each page's text runs are joined with single spaces into one Block; no paragraph segmentation is applied.
Merging emits the extracted (or translated) text as plain text.

doc.overview: The Pensieve TM Filter implements the `IFilter` interface for Pensieve TMs. It allows using a Pensieve TM as an input or output document, similar to other translatable document formats such as TMX, PO, etc. The filter always uses its internal encoding, ignoring any user-specific encoding settings.
limitations: The `setOutput(OutputStream output)` method of `PensiveFilterWriter` is not implemented because Pensieve TM content is binary. You cannot output to other stream types without going through a format conversion.
processingNotes: The filter always uses its internal encoding — any user-specific encoding setting is ignored.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from PHP source files, optionally scoped by inline localization directives.

schema.title: PHP Content Filter
schema.description: Extracts translatable strings from PHP source files

extractOutsideDirectives.title: Extract outside the scope of the directives
extractOutsideDirectives.description: Extract translatable strings found outside directive-controlled regions.

useDirectives.title: Use localization directives
useDirectives.description: Honor //okapi: skip/text directives in PHP source to control extraction scope.

doc.overview:
The **PHP Content** format reads PHP source files and extracts the
translatable text they contain — the text outside PHP code, and text within
regions marked by localization directives. It is intended for PHP templates
and content files where prose is interleaved with code.

Extraction can be scoped with `//okapi:` directives embedded as comments in
the source, which turn extraction on (`text`) and off (`skip`) for regions
of the file. By default these directives are honored, and text outside the
directive-controlled regions is also extracted.

extractOutsideDirectives.help:
When enabled (the default), translatable text found outside the regions
governed by directives is still extracted. Disable it to extract only
the regions explicitly marked by directives.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when directives are honored

useDirectives.help:
When enabled (the default), `//okapi:` directives in the PHP source —
`skip` and `text` — control which regions of the file are extracted.
Disable it to ignore the directives entirely.

limitations: Directive scoping relies on `//okapi:` comments in the source; files without them are extracted according to `extractOutsideDirectives`.
processingNotes: Translatable text becomes blocks; PHP code and structure form the non-translatable skeleton.

examples.title: Only directive-marked regions
examples.description: Extract just the regions a translator has marked with directives.

extractOutsideDirectives.title: Extract outside the scope of the directives

doc.overview: The PHP Content Filter processes PHP source code files, extracting translatable strings from PHP string literals (single-quoted, double-quoted, heredoc, and nowdoc syntax). It is based on the PHP language reference syntax specification. **This filter is not for HTML files with embedded PHP** — it processes only the content within `<?php ... ?>` tags. Many `.php` files are actually HTML with PHP tags and should use the HTML filter instead.

extraction.extractOutsideDirectives.help:
Controls whether translatable strings **outside** the scope of any localization directive are extracted.

- **Enabled**: Strings not covered by a directive are extracted (use directives to *exclude* specific strings)
- **Disabled**: Only strings explicitly within a directive scope are extracted (use directives to *include* specific strings)

This lets you choose the most efficient markup strategy — mark up the minority of strings that differ from the default behavior.
notes: Only takes effect when **Use localization directives** is enabled.

extraction.useDirectives.help:
Enables recognition of localization directives — special comments in the PHP source that override which strings are extracted.

Localization directives let you selectively mark parts of the source for extraction or non-extraction. The directive syntax is shared across all Okapi filters.

If disabled, all directives in the input are ignored and every extractable string is extracted.

inlineCodes.enabled.help:
Activates pattern-based detection of inline codes within extracted text. Matches from the regex rules are converted to inline code placeholders (similar to XLIFF `<ph>` elements), protecting them from translation.

The default patterns detect:
- Partial HTML/XML tags at string boundaries
- Escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`, `\a`, etc.)
- Email addresses
- Template variables in brackets (`[var]`, `{var}`)

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder to simplify translatable segments.

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries out of the translatable text into the non-translatable skeleton.

inlineCodes.rules.help:
Regex patterns that identify inline codes in translatable text. Each match is replaced with a placeholder code in the extracted segment.

Default patterns for PHP content:
```
(\A[^<]*?>)|(<[\w!?/].*?(>|\Z))
|(\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v)
|(\w[-._\w]*\w@\w[-._\w]*\w\.\w{2,3})
|([\[{][\w_$]+?[}\]])
```
examples: `(\\n|\\t)` — matches escape sequences like `\n`, `\t`
`(\w[-._\w]*\w@\w[-._\w]*\w\.\w{2,3})` — matches email addresses
`([\[{][\w_$]+?[}\]])` — matches template variables like `{name}` or `[$var]`

limitations: The `define` statement is not supported — `define('KEY', 'value')` constructs are not extracted.
In array declarations, **both** the string key and string value are extracted, which may result in unwanted items being sent for translation.
processingNotes: **Encoding detection priority**: BOM → `charset` declaration in first 1000 chars → default encoding. If both BOM and `charset` are present but disagree, the BOM encoding wins and a warning is generated.

If the `charset` declaration value is literally the string `charset` (case-insensitive), the file is treated as a template with no encoding declared, and the auto-detected or default encoding is used.
Output `charset` declarations are automatically updated to reflect the selected output encoding.
**UTF-8 BOM handling**: A BOM is included in UTF-8 output only if the input was also UTF-8 *and* had a BOM. Converting from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 never produces a BOM.
Line-break style (CRLF, LF) is preserved from input to output.

examples.title: PHP content with extractable strings
examples.description: Shows which parts of a PHP file are extracted for translation. String literals in assignments, heredoc blocks, and array values are all extractable.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation
simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules

okf_plaintext_regex_lines.sample: $0d$$0a$This is the first sentence. And this is the second one.$0d$$0a$Second paragraph. Each one ends at the line-break.$0d$$0a$$0d$$0a$Third paragraph.$0d$$0a$And the last paragraph may have no line-break.

okf_plaintext_regex_paragraphs.sample: $0d$$0a$This is the first sentence. And this is the second one.$0d$$0a$Second paragraph. Each one ends at the line-break.$0d$$0a$$0d$$0a$Third paragraph.$0d$$0a$And the last paragraph may have no line-break.

doc.overview: The Plain Text filter processes text files encoded in ANSI, Unicode, UTF-8, and UTF-16 with automatic Byte-Order-Mark detection. It provides three extraction modes: **line-based** (`okf_baseplaintext`), **paragraph-based** (`okf_paraplaintext`), and **regex-based** (`okf_regexplaintext`), plus a **spliced lines** variant (`okf_splicedlines`) for source code with line-continuation characters. Each mode shares common parser and inline code settings but exposes different extraction parameters.

extraction.createPlaceholders.help: Creates an inline code placeholder at each position where a splicer character was removed during line merging. This preserves the original line break positions in the translatable content, allowing translators to see where breaks occurred.
notes: Only active in the spliced lines sub-filter (`okf_splicedlines`).

extraction.extractParagraphs.help:
Groups consecutive non-empty lines into paragraph text units, separated by one or more blank lines. Each paragraph becomes a single translatable unit with internal line breaks handled according to `wrapMode`.

When disabled, each line becomes its own text unit.
notes: Only available in the paragraph-based sub-filter (`okf_paraplaintext`).

extraction.regexOptions.help:
Bitmask of `java.util.regex.Pattern` flags to apply to the extraction regex. Common flags:

- **8** = `MULTILINE` — `^` and `$` match line boundaries (not just start/end of input)
- **32** = `DOTALL` — `.` matches any character including newline
- **40** = `MULTILINE | DOTALL` (8 + 32)
extraction.regexOptions.values: Integer bitmask combining `java.util.regex.Pattern` flag constants: `CASE_INSENSITIVE` (2), `MULTILINE` (8), `DOTALL` (32), etc.
examples: `8` — MULTILINE: for line-by-line extraction
`40` — MULTILINE + DOTALL: for paragraph extraction across line boundaries

extraction.rule.help:
Java regular expression used to extract translatable text. The filter applies this regex to the input and creates text units from the matches. Use capturing groups to isolate the translatable portion, then set `sourceGroup` to the group index.

The regex is applied with the flags specified in `regexOptions`.
extraction.rule.values: Java `java.util.regex.Pattern` syntax. Use capturing groups `()` to isolate translatable text.
examples: `(^(?=.+))(.*?)$` — extract each non-empty line (group 2 = content, with MULTILINE flag)

`(
*)(.*?)(

|\Z)` — extract paragraphs separated by blank lines (group 2 = paragraph text, with MULTILINE + DOTALL flags)

extraction.sample.help: Sample text used to test the extraction regex interactively. Extracted matches are shown enclosed in square brackets below the sample. This is for preview/testing only and does not affect processing.

extraction.sourceGroup.help:
The 1-based index of the capturing group in the extraction regex whose match contains the translatable text. Group 0 is the entire match.

For example, with regex `(prefix)(content)(suffix)`, set to `2` to extract only the `content` group.
extraction.sourceGroup.values: Integer index of a capturing group defined in the extraction `rule`. Must be ≥ 0.

extraction.splicer.help:
The character that indicates a line continues on the next line (line-continuation character). When found at the end of a line, the filter merges that line with the following one into a single text unit.

Common splicers: `\` (backslash, used in C/C++/shell), `_` (underscore, used in VB).
extraction.splicer.values: A single character. Common values: `\` (backslash), `_` (underscore), or any custom character.
notes: Only active in the spliced lines sub-filter (`okf_splicedlines`).
examples: `\` — backslash splicer for C/shell continuation lines
`_` — underscore splicer for Visual Basic continuation lines

inlineCodes.enabled.help: Activates regex-based detection of inline codes within extracted text. Any text matching the defined patterns will be converted to inline code placeholders, protecting them from translation.

inlineCodes.rules.help:
Define regex patterns to identify inline codes (placeholders, format specifiers, escape sequences, etc.) within translatable text. Each pattern match is converted to an inline code placeholder.

Default rules typically match C-style format specifiers (`%d`, `%s`) and escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`).
examples: `(\\r\\n)|\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v` — C escape sequences

parser.preserveWS.help: Preserves original whitespace in extracted text units. Typically enabled for paragraph and regex modes where whitespace structure matters.

parser.trimLeading.help: Strips leading spaces and tabs from each extracted text unit. Useful for indented source files where leading whitespace is not part of the translatable content.
notes: For CSV-type usage, this works together with the CSV "Exclude leading/trailing white spaces" option.

parser.trimTrailing.help: Strips trailing spaces and tabs from each extracted text unit. Commonly used to clean up lines with trailing whitespace before translation.
notes: For CSV-type usage, this works together with the CSV "Exclude leading/trailing white spaces" option.

parser.unescapeSource.help: Converts escape sequences (`\t`, `\n`, `\\`, `\uXXXX`) in the source text into their corresponding characters before extraction. Disable if the file contains literal backslash sequences that should be preserved as-is.

parser.wrapMode.help:
Controls how multiple lines are combined into a single text unit when extracting multi-line content:

- **NONE** — Lines are separated by `\n` line feeds (preserves original line structure)
- **SPACES** — Lines are unwrapped and merged with a space between them (useful for reflowable text)
- **PLACEHOLDERS** — Lines are joined with an inline code placeholder representing the original line break (allows translators to reposition breaks)
parser.wrapMode.values: `NONE` = separate with line feeds, `SPACES` = unwrap/merge with spaces, `PLACEHOLDERS` = inline code placeholders for line breaks

processingNotes: The filter auto-detects Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) to determine encoding. If no BOM is found, the configured default encoding is used.
For UTF-8 output: a BOM is written only if the input was UTF-8 *and* had a BOM. Converting from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 produces no BOM.
Output line-break type preserves the original input's line-break convention (CR, LF, or CRLF).
The regex-based sub-filter (`okf_regexplaintext`) detects a wider range of linebreaks at the cost of lower speed and higher memory usage compared to the line-based sub-filter.

examples.title: Line-by-line extraction with trailing trim
examples.description: Uses the base plain text filter to extract each line as a separate text unit, trimming trailing whitespace.

examples.title: Paragraph extraction
examples.description: Groups consecutive non-empty lines into paragraph text units. Paragraphs are separated by one or more blank lines.
examples.input:
```text
This is the first sentence.
And this continues the paragraph.

This is a new paragraph.
```
examples.output:
```text
Text Unit 1: "This is the first sentence.\nAnd this continues the paragraph."
Text Unit 2: "This is a new paragraph."
```

examples.title: Regex extraction by line
examples.description: Uses a regex rule with MULTILINE flag to extract each non-empty line. Group 2 captures the line content.

examples.title: Spliced lines with backslash continuation
examples.description: Merges lines ending with backslash into single text units, inserting inline code placeholders where splicers were removed.
examples.input:
```text
This line continues \
on the next line.
This is separate.
```
examples.output:
```text
Text Unit 1: "This line continues <x/>on the next line."
Text Unit 2: "This is separate."
```

okf_plaintext_regex_paragraphs.sample: $0d$$0a$This is the first sentence. And this is the second one.$0d$$0a$Second paragraph. Each one ends at the line-break.$0d$$0a$$0d$$0a$Third paragraph.$0d$$0a$And the last paragraph may have no line-break.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from plain `.txt` files, one block per line or per paragraph.

schema.title: Plain Text Format
schema.description: Configuration for the plain text format reader/writer

segmentByLine.description: If true, each line becomes a separate block. If false, paragraphs (separated by blank lines) are blocks.

doc.overview:
The plain text format reads generic `.txt` documents and emits the
text content as translatable blocks. It is the fallback for files that
carry no markup or structure of their own — release notes, prompts,
word lists, or any free-form text that needs translating.

By default the reader segments by line: each non-empty line becomes one
block, and blank lines and the original line endings (LF, CRLF, or bare
CR) are preserved as skeleton so the document round-trips after merge.
Switching to paragraph mode groups runs of lines separated by blank
lines into a single block, which suits prose where sentences span
several wrapped lines. This mirrors Okapi's plain-text filter.

segmentByLine.description: Treat each line as a block, rather than each paragraph.
segmentByLine.help:
When enabled (the default), every non-empty line of the input
becomes its own block. Blank lines and line terminators are kept as
skeleton and are not extracted.

When disabled, the reader groups consecutive lines into paragraphs —
a block ends at a blank line. Use paragraph mode for prose that wraps
across multiple physical lines, where a sentence should stay in one
block rather than being split per line.

processingNotes: LF, CRLF, and bare CR are all recognised as line terminators and preserved.
Blank lines emit a non-translatable data part; they are never extracted as blocks.

examples.title: Paragraph mode
examples.description: Group wrapped lines into paragraph blocks instead of one block per line.

For UTF-8 output: a BOM is written only if the input was UTF-8 *and* had a BOM. Converting from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 produces no BOM.
Output line-break type preserves the original input's line-break convention (CR, LF, or CRLF).
The regex-based sub-filter (`okf_regexplaintext`) detects a wider range of linebreaks at the cost of lower speed and higher memory usage compared to the line-based sub-filter.

```
examples.output:

Text Unit 1: "This is the first sentence.\nAnd this continues the paragraph."
Text Unit 2: "This is a new paragraph."
```

on the next line.

```

Text Unit 1: "This line continues <x/>on the next line."
Text Unit 2: "This is separate."
```

doc.overview: The Plain Text filter processes text files encoded in ANSI, Unicode, UTF-8, and UTF-16 with automatic Byte-Order-Mark detection. It provides three extraction modes: **line-based** (`okf_baseplaintext`), **paragraph-based** (`okf_paraplaintext`), and **regex-based** (`okf_regexplaintext`), plus a **spliced lines** variant (`okf_splicedlines`) for source code with line-continuation characters. Each mode shares common parser and inline code settings but exposes different extraction parameters.

extraction.createPlaceholders.help: Creates an inline code placeholder at each position where a splicer character was removed during line merging. This preserves the original line break positions in the translatable content, allowing translators to see where breaks occurred.
notes: Only active in the spliced lines sub-filter (`okf_splicedlines`).

extraction.extractParagraphs.help:
Groups consecutive non-empty lines into paragraph text units, separated by one or more blank lines. Each paragraph becomes a single translatable unit with internal line breaks handled according to `wrapMode`.

When disabled, each line becomes its own text unit.
notes: Only available in the paragraph-based sub-filter (`okf_paraplaintext`).

extraction.regexOptions.help:
Bitmask of `java.util.regex.Pattern` flags to apply to the extraction regex. Common flags:

- **8** = `MULTILINE` — `^` and `$` match line boundaries (not just start/end of input)
- **32** = `DOTALL` — `.` matches any character including newline
- **40** = `MULTILINE | DOTALL` (8 + 32)
extraction.regexOptions.values: Integer bitmask combining `java.util.regex.Pattern` flag constants: `CASE_INSENSITIVE` (2), `MULTILINE` (8), `DOTALL` (32), etc.
examples: `8` — MULTILINE: for line-by-line extraction
`40` — MULTILINE + DOTALL: for paragraph extraction across line boundaries

extraction.rule.help:
Java regular expression used to extract translatable text. The filter applies this regex to the input and creates text units from the matches. Use capturing groups to isolate the translatable portion, then set `sourceGroup` to the group index.

The regex is applied with the flags specified in `regexOptions`.
extraction.rule.values: Java `java.util.regex.Pattern` syntax. Use capturing groups `()` to isolate translatable text.
examples: `(^(?=.+))(.*?)$` — extract each non-empty line (group 2 = content, with MULTILINE flag)

`(
*)(.*?)(

|\Z)` — extract paragraphs separated by blank lines (group 2 = paragraph text, with MULTILINE + DOTALL flags)

extraction.sample.help: Sample text used to test the extraction regex interactively. Extracted matches are shown enclosed in square brackets below the sample. This is for preview/testing only and does not affect processing.

extraction.sourceGroup.help:
The 1-based index of the capturing group in the extraction regex whose match contains the translatable text. Group 0 is the entire match.

For example, with regex `(prefix)(content)(suffix)`, set to `2` to extract only the `content` group.
extraction.sourceGroup.values: Integer index of a capturing group defined in the extraction `rule`. Must be ≥ 0.

extraction.splicer.help:
The character that indicates a line continues on the next line (line-continuation character). When found at the end of a line, the filter merges that line with the following one into a single text unit.

Common splicers: `\` (backslash, used in C/C++/shell), `_` (underscore, used in VB).
extraction.splicer.values: A single character. Common values: `\` (backslash), `_` (underscore), or any custom character.
notes: Only active in the spliced lines sub-filter (`okf_splicedlines`).
examples: `\` — backslash splicer for C/shell continuation lines
`_` — underscore splicer for Visual Basic continuation lines

inlineCodes.enabled.help: Activates regex-based detection of inline codes within extracted text. Any text matching the defined patterns will be converted to inline code placeholders, protecting them from translation.

inlineCodes.rules.help:
Define regex patterns to identify inline codes (placeholders, format specifiers, escape sequences, etc.) within translatable text. Each pattern match is converted to an inline code placeholder.

Default rules typically match C-style format specifiers (`%d`, `%s`) and escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`).
examples: `(\\r\\n)|\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v` — C escape sequences

parser.preserveWS.help: Preserves original whitespace in extracted text units. Typically enabled for paragraph and regex modes where whitespace structure matters.

parser.trimLeading.help: Strips leading spaces and tabs from each extracted text unit. Useful for indented source files where leading whitespace is not part of the translatable content.
notes: For CSV-type usage, this works together with the CSV "Exclude leading/trailing white spaces" option.

parser.trimTrailing.help: Strips trailing spaces and tabs from each extracted text unit. Commonly used to clean up lines with trailing whitespace before translation.
notes: For CSV-type usage, this works together with the CSV "Exclude leading/trailing white spaces" option.

parser.unescapeSource.help: Converts escape sequences (`\t`, `\n`, `\\`, `\uXXXX`) in the source text into their corresponding characters before extraction. Disable if the file contains literal backslash sequences that should be preserved as-is.

parser.wrapMode.help:
Controls how multiple lines are combined into a single text unit when extracting multi-line content:

- **NONE** — Lines are separated by `\n` line feeds (preserves original line structure)
- **SPACES** — Lines are unwrapped and merged with a space between them (useful for reflowable text)
- **PLACEHOLDERS** — Lines are joined with an inline code placeholder representing the original line break (allows translators to reposition breaks)
parser.wrapMode.values: `NONE` = separate with line feeds, `SPACES` = unwrap/merge with spaces, `PLACEHOLDERS` = inline code placeholders for line breaks

processingNotes: The filter auto-detects Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) to determine encoding. If no BOM is found, the configured default encoding is used.
For UTF-8 output: a BOM is written only if the input was UTF-8 *and* had a BOM. Converting from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 produces no BOM.
Output line-break type preserves the original input's line-break convention (CR, LF, or CRLF).
The regex-based sub-filter (`okf_regexplaintext`) detects a wider range of linebreaks at the cost of lower speed and higher memory usage compared to the line-based sub-filter.

entries.description: Read and write GNU gettext PO and POT catalogues, extracting each message as a translatable block.

schema.title: PO Format (GNU Gettext)
schema.description: Configuration for the PO/POT format reader/writer

allowEmptyOutputTarget.title: Allow Empty Target Output
allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: Allow empty target in output when no translation is available

bilingualMode.title: Bilingual Mode
bilingualMode.description: In bilingual mode (default), msgid is the source text and msgstr is the target. In monolingual mode, msgid is treated as an ID.

codeFinderRules.title: Code Finder Rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

preserveUntranslated.title: Preserve Untranslated Entries
preserveUntranslated.description: If true, entries with empty msgstr are emitted as blocks. If false, they are skipped.

useCodeFinder.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection within PO message text

wrapContent.title: Wrap Long Content Lines
wrapContent.description: Wrap long content lines in output at approximately 80 characters

doc.overview:
PO (Portable Object) is the GNU gettext catalogue format. A `.po` file
pairs `msgid` source strings with `msgstr` translations, optionally
disambiguated by `msgctxt`, with plural forms via `msgid_plural` and
`msgstr[N]`, translator and extracted comments, source references, and
`#, fuzzy` flags. `.pot` templates carry the same structure with empty
translations.

The reader emits one translatable block per message: in the default
bilingual mode the `msgid` is the source text and the `msgstr` is the
target. In monolingual mode the `msgid` is treated purely as an
identifier and the `msgstr` provides the source text instead — the
shape used by catalogues that ship message IDs rather than English
strings. Comments, context, and references are preserved on the block
and written back. This mirrors Okapi's PO filter.

allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: Write an empty target when no translation is available.
allowEmptyOutputTarget.help:
A writer-side option. When enabled, a message with no available
translation is written with an empty `msgstr` rather than being
dropped or filled with the source. When disabled (the default),
empty targets are not emitted.

bilingualMode.description: Treat msgid as the source text and msgstr as the target.
bilingualMode.help:
When enabled (the default), each message is read bilingually: the
`msgid` is the source text and the `msgstr` is the target
translation. When disabled (monolingual mode), the `msgid` is used
only as an identifier and the `msgstr` is taken as the source text —
the layout used by catalogues whose message IDs are symbolic rather
than English.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in message text.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within a translatable
block is protected as an inline code rather than translated. Used to
shield gettext placeholders or format specifiers. Only takes effect
when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.
examples: `%[0-9]*[sd]` — protect `printf`-style format specifiers
`\{[0-9]+\}` — protect numbered placeholders like `{0}`

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

preserveUntranslated.description: Emit blocks for messages that have an empty msgstr.
preserveUntranslated.help:
Controls whether messages with an empty `msgstr` (untranslated
entries) are emitted as blocks. When enabled (the default), they are
kept so the full catalogue is visible for translation. When
disabled, untranslated entries are skipped.

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside message text using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes (placeholders,
variables, format specifiers) within the message text, using the
patterns in `codeFinderRules`. When disabled (the default), no
pattern-based inline codes are extracted.

wrapContent.description: Wrap long content lines in the output catalogue.
wrapContent.help:
A writer-side option. When enabled (the default), long `msgid` and
`msgstr` values are wrapped across multiple lines at roughly 80
characters, matching conventional gettext formatting. When disabled,
each value is written on a single line. The reader ignores this
setting.

processingNotes: One block is emitted per message; `msgctxt` is stored on the block context, and comments and references are preserved.
Plural forms (`msgid_plural` / `msgstr[N]`) and `#, fuzzy` flags are recognised and round-tripped.

examples.description: Treat msgid as an identifier and msgstr as the source text.

examples.title: Skip untranslated entries
examples.description: Extract only messages that already carry a translation.

doc.overview: The PO Filter processes Gettext PO (Portable Object) and POT (PO Template) resource files. It supports both **bilingual mode** (where `msgid` is the source and `msgstr` is the translation) and **monolingual mode** (where `msgid` is a real identifier and `msgstr` is the text). The filter handles plural forms, domains, fuzzy flags, various comment types, and automatic encoding detection from the PO header's `charset` declaration.

identification.includeMsgContextInNote.help:
Includes the content of the `msgctxt` line as a localization note attached to the extracted text unit.

This makes the context visible to translators in tools that display notes but may not show the PO context field directly.

identification.makeID.help:
Generates identifiers from the **hash code** of the source text (`msgid`), optionally combined with the domain prefix and `msgctxt` value.

The generated ID is set as the resource name. Two entries with the same source text but different `msgctxt` values will get different identifiers.

If a `msgctxt` is in the Okapi format `okpCtx:tu=123`, the text unit ID is set to the value specified (e.g., `123`).
notes: The generated ID may not be unique if the source text is identical within the same domain and has no distinct context values.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when bilingual mode is enabled

inlineCodes.enabled.help:
Enables pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, escape sequences, etc.) within translatable text. Matched patterns are converted to inline code placeholders that translators can reposition but not modify.

The default patterns detect:
- **printf-style placeholders**: `%s`, `%d`, `%2$s`, `%-10.2f`
- **Escape sequences**: `\n`, `\r`, `\t`, `\a`, `\b`, `\f`, `\v`, `\r\n`
- **Numbered placeholders**: `{0}`, `{1,date}`, etc.

inlineCodes.mergeAdjacent.help:
When multiple inline codes appear next to each other with no translatable text between them, they are merged into a single placeholder.

This simplifies the segment for translators by reducing the number of code placeholders they need to manage.

inlineCodes.moveBoundaryCodes.help:
Moves inline codes that appear at the very beginning or end of a segment out of the translatable content and into the skeleton (non-translatable framework).

This is useful when leading/trailing codes (like `\n` at the end of a string) don't need to be visible to translators.

inlineCodes.rules.help:
Regex patterns that identify inline codes in translatable text. Each pattern is a Java regular expression tested against extracted text — any match becomes an inline code placeholder.

The default rule set for PO files:
```
((%(([-0+#]?)[-0+#]?)((\d\$)?)([\d\*]*(\.[\d\*]*)?)[dioxXucsfeEgGpn])
|((\\r\\n)|\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v)
|(\{\d.*?\}))
```

This matches printf format specifiers, C escape sequences, and Java MessageFormat placeholders.
inlineCodes.rules.values: Each rule is a Java `java.util.regex.Pattern` compatible regular expression.

mode.bilingualMode.help:
In **bilingual mode**, `msgid` is the source text and `msgstr` is the translation — this is the standard PO file layout.

In **monolingual mode** (when disabled), `msgid` is treated as a real identifier (not source text), and `msgstr` contains the translatable text. The `msgid` value becomes the resource name.

Most PO files are bilingual. Use monolingual mode only when your PO workflow uses `msgid` as a key/identifier rather than human-readable source text.

mode.protectApproved.help:
Marks all entries that are **not empty and not fuzzy** with a non-translatable flag, preventing them from being modified during translation.

This is useful when you want to re-process a PO file but only allow changes to entries that are still fuzzy or untranslated, leaving already-approved translations untouched.
notes: The fuzzy flag (`#, fuzzy`) in PO files indicates a proposed translation. Entries **without** this flag and with non-empty `msgstr` are considered approved.

mode.wrapContent.help:
Controls whether long content lines in `msgid` and `msgstr` entries are wrapped across multiple quoted strings in the output.

PO files conventionally wrap long strings with an empty first line followed by continuation lines:
```po
msgid ""
"This is a long "
"wrapped string"
```

Disabling this outputs each entry as a single line.

output.allowEmptyOutputTarget.help:
Allows writing empty `msgstr` entries in the output when no translation is available.

By default, entries without translations may be handled differently (e.g., copying the source). Enable this to explicitly permit empty targets in the output file.

output.outputGeneric.help:
Outputs the PO file in a generic (normalized) format rather than preserving the original formatting style.

Useful when you want consistent output formatting regardless of how the input file was structured.

limitations: None known per the official documentation.
processingNotes: **Encoding detection priority**: BOM → `charset` declaration in first 1000 chars → default encoding. If BOM and `charset` conflict, BOM wins with a warning.
The PO header `charset` declaration is **automatically updated** in output to match the selected output encoding.
**No language information** is updated in the PO header — language-related fields must be updated manually.
Output line-breaks match the input file's line-break style.
Plural forms (`msgstr[0]`, `msgstr[1]`, ...) must appear in **sequential order** and are grouped as `x-gettext-plurals`.
Domains are represented as groups with type `x-gettext-domain`.
The **fuzzy flag** (`#, fuzzy`) maps to the `approved` target property: fuzzy → `approved=no`; removing or changing `approved` removes the fuzzy flag from output.
Lines without quotes on their first line (e.g., bare `msgid` followed by quoted continuation) are normalized to include an empty initial string.
Comment types supported: extracted (`#.` → note), translator (`# ` → transnote), reference (`#:` → references). Context comments (`#|`) are currently ignored.
The `msgctxt` value is used as a differentiator for generated identifiers — entries with the same source but different contexts get different IDs.
If `msgctxt` matches the Okapi format `okpCtx:tu=NNN`, the text unit ID is set to the specified value.

examples.title: Standard bilingual PO file
examples.description: A typical PO file with header, simple entries, and plural forms. The filter extracts `msgid` as source and `msgstr` as target in bilingual mode.
examples.input:
```po
# PO file for myApp

msgid ""
msgstr ""
"Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8\n"
"Plural-Forms: nplurals=2; plural=(n != 1);\n"

msgid "diverging after version %d of %s"
msgstr ""

msgid "You have selected %d file for deletion"
msgid_plural "You have selected %d files for deletion"
msgstr[0] ""
msgstr[1] ""
```

examples.title: Domain grouping
examples.description: PO files can use `domain` directives to partition entries. The filter represents each domain as a group with type `x-gettext-domain`.
examples.input:
```po
domain TheDomain1
msgid "Text 1 in domain 'TheDomain1'"
msgstr "Texte 1 dans le domain 'TheDomain1'"
```

examples.title: Context-based ID assignment
examples.description: When `msgctxt` uses the Okapi format `okpCtx:tu=NNN`, the filter sets the text unit ID to the specified value.
examples.input:
```po
msgctxt "okpCtx:tu=123"
msgid "Source"
msgstr "Target"
```

examples.description: Configure the filter for monolingual PO files where `msgid` is a key identifier, not human-readable source text.

useKeyCondition.title: Enable Key Condition
useKeyCondition.description: Filter entries for extraction based on a regex condition applied to their keys.

useLd.title: Use Localization Directives
useLd.description: Enable recognition of localization directives in comments to override default extraction behavior.

escapeExtendedChars.title: Escape Extended Characters
escapeExtendedChars.description: Escape extended Unicode characters in output.

useJavaEscapes.title: Retain Java Escapes
useJavaEscapes.description: Keep Java property escape sequences in extracted text instead of resolving them.

convertLFandTab.title: Convert \n and \t
convertLFandTab.description: Convert \n and \t escape sequences to actual line-breaks and tabs during processing.

doc.overview: The Properties Filter processes Java `.properties` files, extracting key-value pairs as translatable text units. It is based on the `java.util.Properties` specification and supports additional comment styles for compatibility with other properties-like formats. The filter supports localization directives, key-based filtering, sub-filters for embedded content (e.g., HTML), and inline code detection via regex patterns.

commentsAreNotes.description: Attach preceding comments to each entry as translator notes.

convertLFandTab.description: Convert \n and \t escape sequences to actual line-breaks and tabs during processing.

escapeExtendedChars.description: Convert all characters above U+007F to \uHHHH escape sequences in the output.

extraComments.description: Recognize semicolon and double-slash comment styles in addition to standard # and ! markers.

extractOnlyMatchingKey.description: When enabled, extract only entries whose keys match the condition; otherwise exclude matching keys.

extraction.extraComments.help:
Recognizes additional comment styles beyond strict Java properties comments (`#` and `!`):

- Lines starting with `;`
- Lines where `//` is the first non-whitespace sequence

`//` after a `=` is treated as part of the value, not a comment.

extraction.extractOnlyMatchingKey.help:
Controls the key filtering behavior:

- **true** — Extract **only** items with keys matching the pattern
- **false** — Extract all items **except** those with keys matching the pattern

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when key condition is enabled

extraction.keyCondition.help:
Java regular expression tested against property keys to determine extraction.

The pattern uses `java.util.regex.Pattern` syntax.
examples: `.*text.*` — matches keys containing "text" (e.g., `text.err1`, `menu_text_file`)
`^msg\.` — matches keys starting with "msg."
`^(?!internal\.).*` — matches all keys except those starting with "internal."

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when key condition is enabled

extraction.localizeOutside.help:
Controls whether items **not** within the scope of a localization directive are extracted.

- **Enabled**: items outside directive scope are extracted (use directives only to *exclude*)
- **Disabled**: only items explicitly within directive scope are extracted (use directives to *include*)

This lets you mark up fewer parts of the source — choose the minority case for your directives.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when localization directives are enabled

extraction.useKeyCondition.help:
Enables filtering of entries based on their property key. When enabled, only entries whose keys match (or don't match) the `keyCondition` regex are extracted.

Localization directives take precedence over key conditions.
notes: Localization directives have precedence over key conditions.

idLikeResname.description: Use the property key value as the text unit ID in addition to the resource name.

identification.idLikeResname.help:
Uses the property key as the text unit ID. Note that the key is **already** extracted into the `name` property (`resname` in XLIFF) regardless of this setting.

Some output formats have restrictions on ID values — for example, XLIFF 2.0 requires IDs to be NMTOKEN values, which property keys may not satisfy.
notes: The key is always available as `resname` regardless of this setting.
Property keys may not be valid IDs for all output formats (e.g., XLIFF2 requires NMTOKEN).

identification.useLd.help:
Enables recognition of localization directives — special comments that override default extraction behavior. When disabled, any directives in the input are ignored.

Localization directives have **precedence over key conditions**. Their syntax is shared across all Okapi filters.
notes: Directives override key conditions when both are active.

inlineCodes.enabled.help:
Enables pattern-based detection of inline codes in extracted text. Matches are converted to inline code placeholders, protecting them from translation.

The default patterns detect:
- `printf`-style format specifiers (`%s`, `%d`, `%2$s`)
- Escape sequences (`\n`, `\r`, `\t`, etc.)
- Java `MessageFormat` placeholders (`{0}`, `{1,date}`)

keyCondition.description: Regular expression pattern to test against property keys for extraction filtering.

localizeOutside.description: Extract translatable items that are not within the scope of a localization directive.

notes.commentsAreNotes.help:
Attaches comments preceding each entry as a **note** property on the corresponding text unit. All comment lines before an entry are grouped into a single note.

Useful for providing translators with context about the purpose of each string.

output.convertLFandTab.help:
Converts `\n` and `\t` escape sequences to actual line-breaks and tabs during processing. All other escaped characters (e.g., `\\`, `\:`, `\=`) remain escaped.

Disable this if your properties values use `\n` and `\t` as literal text rather than control characters.

output.escapeExtendedChars.help:
Converts all characters above U+007F to Unicode escape sequences (`\uHHHH`) in the output.

When disabled, only characters not supported by the output encoding are escaped. Enable this for strict Java properties compatibility where the file must be ASCII-safe.

output.useJavaEscapes.help: Retains Java property escape sequences in extracted text rather than converting them. When disabled (default), escapes are resolved to their actual characters during extraction.

useJavaEscapes.description: Keep Java property escape sequences in extracted text instead of resolving them.

useKeyCondition.description: Filter entries for extraction based on a regex condition applied to their keys.

useLd.description: Enable recognition of localization directives in comments to override default extraction behavior.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: BOM handling: if the input has a Unicode BOM, the corresponding encoding is used. Output BOM is only preserved for UTF-8 → UTF-8 conversions where the input had a BOM.
Line-breaks in the output match the original input format.
Each property entry maps to one text unit: key → name (`resname`), value → source content, preceding comments → note (if enabled).
Java properties in XML format should be processed with the XML Filter or XML Stream Filter, not this filter.

examples.title: Basic properties file
examples.description: A simple Java properties file showing the two key-value separator styles. Both `=` and `:` are supported.
examples.input:
```properties
# This is a comment

labelOK= OK
msgBadFile: Invalid input file
```

examples.title: Key-based filtering
examples.description: Using the key condition `.*text.*` with **Extract only matching keys** enabled. Only keys containing "text" are extracted for translation.
examples.input:
```properties
key1 = Text for key1
text.err1 = Text for text.err1
menu_text_file = Text for menu_text_file
```
examples.output:
```
Extracted: text.err1, menu_text_file
Skipped: key1
```

examples.title: HTML sub-filter configuration
examples.description: Using the `okf_properties-html-subfilter` configuration to process property values containing HTML markup through the HTML filter.

entries.description: Read and write Qt Linguist TS (.ts) files, the bilingual XML used by Qt's lupdate and lrelease.

doc.overview:
Qt Linguist TS is the bilingual XML format used by Qt's `lupdate` and
`lrelease` tools to carry UI translations. A `<TS>` document groups
one or more `<context>` elements; each context groups `<message>`
entries with a `<source>` and a `<translation>`. Translation state is
encoded on the `<translation>` element's `type` attribute — no type
means approved, `unfinished` means pending review, and `obsolete`
means the entry is unused. Plural messages (`numerus="yes"`) carry one
`<numerusform>` per locale plural form. Inline non-XML bytes appear as
`<byte value="…"/>` elements.

The reader extracts each message's source as a translatable block,
matched with its existing translation, and preserves the context and
message structure for the writer. The format is detected by sniffing
for a `<TS>` … `</TS>` document rather than by the `.ts` extension,
which collides with TypeScript.
limitations: The format is not auto-detected by the `.ts` file extension (it conflicts with TypeScript); detection relies on sniffing the `<TS>` root element or an explicit format declaration.
Non-XML bytes (`<byte value="…"/>` elements) are surfaced as inline placeholder runs and preserved through exchange; they are not decoded to their raw character equivalents on read.
There are no configurable parameters; behaviour follows the default contract.
processingNotes: One translatable block per `<message>`; context and message structure round-trip as skeleton.
Plural messages carry one `<numerusform>` per locale plural form.
Translation state (approved / unfinished / obsolete) is read from the `<translation>` `type` attribute.

examples.title: Translate a Qt UI catalogue
examples.description:
No configuration is required. Declare the format to extract each
`<message>` source and merge translations back into the `.ts`
document.

openManifest.title: Open the manifest file before processing

doc.overview: The Rainbow Translation Kit Filter implements the `IFilter` interface for reading packages generated by the Rainbow Translation Kit Creation Step. It processes translation kit packages by reading their `manifest.rkm` file. This is a **read-only** filter — it cannot re-write translation kits, only read them for input processing.

general.openManifest.help: An interactive option that, when enabled (the default), opens the package's `manifest.rkm` in an editor dialog before processing so a user can review or trim the file list — for example to merge back only a subset of the kit. It drives a Swing/SWT dialog and has no effect when the filter runs without a UI; in a headless or automated context leave it at the default and edit the manifest beforehand if selective processing is needed.
notes: Requires a graphical environment; the dialog is skipped where no UI is available.
The manifest file (`manifest.rkm`) is the entry point for the entire translation kit package — it must be the input file used to process the package.

limitations: This is a **read-only** filter. It does not generate output corresponding to the input — it must be used for input only.
Cannot re-write or regenerate translation kit packages.
processingNotes: Rainbow translation kits are directory-based packages with a specific layout. The `manifest.rkm` file in the directory root serves as the entry point for processing the entire package.
Different types of translation kit packages exist, each with its own directory structure and content.

examples.title: Processing a translation kit
examples.description: To process a Rainbow translation kit, point the filter at the `manifest.rkm` file inside the kit directory — not at individual files within the package.
examples.input:
```
myproject-tkit/
├── manifest.rkm      ← use this as input
├── original/
│   └── ...
└── work/
    └── ...
```

entries.description: Extract translatable strings from arbitrary text formats using configurable regular-expression rules.

doc.overview:
The regex format is a configurable extractor for text formats that
have no dedicated reader — Mac `.strings`, INI files, StringInfo, and
similar key/value or delimited text. You supply a list of rules; each
rule is a regular expression with capture groups that identify the
translatable source text and, optionally, an identifier and a note.

Rules are applied across the input left to right. Where rules would
overlap, the earliest match wins. Each match produces one translatable
block whose source is the configured capture group; the text on either
side of the captured value is preserved verbatim so the writer can
rebuild the line by assembling prefix, translated value, and suffix.
Content that no rule matches is preserved as non-translatable
structure. With no rules configured, nothing is extracted and the
whole input is preserved.

Configuration is supplied on the collection's `format:` block in a
`.kapi` project. The keys are: `rules` — a list of
`{ pattern, sourceGroup, idGroup, noteGroup }` records, where
`sourceGroup` is the 1-based capture group holding the source text
(required, must be at least 1), `idGroup` names the block
(`0` auto-generates IDs), and `noteGroup` captures a translator note
(`0` for none); `escapeType` — how to decode escapes inside the
extracted text, one of `none` (the default), `backslash` (`\"`, `\\`,
`\n`, `\t`, …), or `doublechar` (a character escaped by doubling it);
and `escapeChar` — the character used for `doublechar` mode (default
`"`).
limitations: Patterns use Go's RE2 regular-expression syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookarounds.
Where matches overlap, only the first match is kept; later overlapping matches are discarded.
Escape decoding (`escapeType`) applies to the extracted source text, which can differ from how a delimiter-scanning filter would handle the same escapes.
processingNotes: One block per rule match; non-matching spans become non-translatable Data so the output round-trips.
The raw text surrounding the captured source group is stored on the block so the writer reassembles output by `prefix + value + suffix`.
The optional `idGroup` and `noteGroup` captures populate the block name and a translator note respectively.

examples.title: Extract Mac .strings entries
examples.description:
A single rule capturing the value of a `"key" = "value";` line.
Group 1 is the source text; group 2 supplies the block ID.
examples.config:
format:
  name: regex
  config:
    rules:
      - pattern: '"([^"]*)"\s*=\s*"([^"]*)";'
        sourceGroup: 2
        idGroup: 1
    escapeType: backslash

okf_regex-srt.rule0.sample: 1$0a$00:00:06,020 --> 00:00:07,020$0a$Hi, everyone.$0a$this is more$0a$$0a$2$0a$00:00:07,020 --> 00:00:08,020$0a$My name is Randy Becker.$0a$$0a$3$0a$00:00:08,020 --> 00:00:11,000$0a$I'm an application engineer at Keysight Technologies.

okf_regex-macStrings.rule0.sample: /* Menu item to make the current document plain text */$0a$"Make Plain Text" = "Make Plain \"Text";$0a$/* Menu item to make the current document rich text */$0a$"Make Rich Text" = "Make Rich Text";$0a$/* An unquoted key */$0a$NSSomeKey = "Foobar";
okf_regex-macStrings.rule1.sample: /* An unquoted key */$0a$NSSomeKey = "Foobar";$0a$/* Another unquoted key */$0a$NSSomeKey2 = "Bizbaz";
okf_regex-macStrings.rule4.sample: /* Menu item to make the current document plain text */$0a$"Make Plain Text" = "Make Plain \"Text";$0a$/* Menu item to make the current document rich text */$0a$"Make Rich Text" = "Make Rich Text";$0a$
okf_regex-macStrings.rule5.sample: /* An unquoted key */$0a$NSSomeKey = "Foobar";$0a$/* Another unquoted key */$0a$NSSomeKey2 = "Bizbaz";

doc.overview: The Regex Filter extracts translatable text from any text-based format using user-defined regular expressions with capturing groups. Each rule pairs a regex pattern with an action (extract content, extract strings, treat as comment, start/end section, or skip), and capturing groups map matched text to source, target, identifier, and note roles. The filter processes rules in order, scanning the document from top to bottom and applying the first matching rule at each position.

endString.description: Character(s) marking the end of a string for string extraction.
endString.help: Defines the character(s) that mark the end of a string. Must have the same number of characters as **Beginning of string**, with each position corresponding to its matching start character.
endString.values: One or more single characters, positionally paired with start characters.

dependsOn.condition: must have equal number of characters, positionally paired

extraction.oneLevelGroups.help:
Flattens section nesting. When a **Start a section** rule fires while a section is already open, the open section (and any nested sections) is closed first, so sections never nest — each new section ends the previous one. This is the mechanism behind the auto-close behaviour: with it enabled you only write **Start a section** rules and omit the matching **End a section** rules.

When disabled, sections nest normally and every **Start a section** rule must be balanced by a corresponding **End a section** rule.
notes: When enabled, do not define explicit End a section rules — starts auto-close prior sections.

extraction.regexOptions.help:
Raw integer bit-mask of `java.util.regex.Pattern` flags applied when **every** rule pattern is compiled. The flags affect all rules uniformly; per-rule control is only possible with the inline `(?idmsux-idmsux)` flag construct inside an individual pattern.

The UI normally exposes this through the **Dot matches line-feed**, **Multi-line**, and **Ignore case** toggles, which set or clear individual bits. Set the integer directly only when you need flags those toggles do not cover (e.g. `UNICODE_CASE`, `COMMENTS`).
extraction.regexOptions.values: Integer sum of `Pattern` flag constants. Default `40` = `Pattern.DOTALL` (32) `|` `Pattern.MULTILINE` (8). Common bits: `CASE_INSENSITIVE`=2, `MULTILINE`=8, `DOTALL`=32, `UNICODE_CASE`=64, `COMMENTS`=4.

mimeType.description: MIME type used to identify the document format during extraction.
mimeType.help: The MIME type identifies the document format and may affect how translated text is written back. For most use cases, `text/plain` is appropriate.

output.localizeOutside.help: Determines whether translatable items that fall **outside** the scope of any localization directive are still extracted. This is the persisted form of the directives' `localizeOutside` flag and only has an effect when **Use localization directives** is enabled. Letting content outside directives be localized lets you annotate only the exceptions you want to *exclude*, rather than marking every translatable segment.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this option to take effect

output.removeBSlashEscape.help: On import, decodes C-style escape sequences inside an extracted string into their literal characters: `\n` → line feed, `\r` → carriage return, `\t` → tab, and `\\` → a single backslash. Use this so translators see and edit real line breaks and tabs rather than the literal escape text. Disabled by default, which preserves the escape sequences verbatim in the extracted source.

output.useBSlashEscape.help: Affects the **Extract the strings in the source group** action. When enabled, a backslash inside a quoted string escapes the following character (e.g. `\"`, `\'`, `\\`), so an escaped delimiter does not terminate the string and the scanner steps over the escaped pair. Enabled by default. Disable it for formats where the backslash is an ordinary literal character.

dependsOn.condition: the two escape mechanisms are independent flags; typically only one applies to a given format

output.useLd.help: Enables recognition of localization directives embedded in the input. This is the persisted form of the localization-directives `useLD` flag; when disabled, all directives in the document are ignored. Exposed in the UI as **Use localization directives**.

startString.description: Character(s) marking the start of a string for string extraction.
startString.help:
Defines the character(s) that mark the beginning of a string, used by the **Extract the strings in the source group** action.

Multiple characters define multiple alternative string delimiters. Each character position must correspond to its matching end character.
startString.values: One or more single characters. E.g., `"` or `"'` (double-quote and single-quote as two alternatives).
examples: `"` — strings delimited by double quotes
`"'` — strings delimited by either `"` or `'` (pair with `"'` in End of string)

subfilters.subFilter.help: Name of a filter configuration to run **on the matched source group content** as a sub-filter. When set, instead of emitting the matched source as a single text unit, the regex filter opens the named filter on that text and merges its events back, with a reference event linking the sub-document to the host text unit. Use it when a captured value is itself a structured fragment — for example a regex rule isolates a value that contains HTML, and `okf_html` is named here to extract the translatable text from inside it. Leave empty for no sub-filtering.
subfilters.subFilter.values: Filter configuration identifier (e.g. `okf_html`, `okf_json`). Default is empty (no sub-filter).

useDoubleCharEscape.description: Use character doubling to escape characters in strings.
useDoubleCharEscape.help: Characters escaped by doubling (e.g., `""`) are treated as literal content, not string delimiters. Used by the **Extract the strings in the source group** action.

dependsOn.condition: the two escape mechanisms are independent flags; typically only one applies to a given format

limitations: The **entire document is loaded into memory** to apply regular expressions — very large documents may cause memory issues.
The **Extract strings outside the rules** option is not yet implemented.
The filter does not recognize encoding declarations in the document and therefore cannot update them automatically.
processingNotes: **Encoding detection**: Unicode BOM is detected and used automatically; otherwise falls back to the default encoding specified when opening the document.
**BOM handling**: UTF-8 output includes a BOM only if the input was also UTF-8 and had a BOM. Transcoding from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 never adds a BOM.
**Line-breaks**: Output preserves the same line-break style as the input.
**Parsing order**: Rules are evaluated in list order at each position; the first match wins and the search position advances to the end of that match.
**Regex options**: `dotAll`, `multiLine`, and `ignoreCase` apply globally to all rules. Per-rule overrides are possible using the `(?idmsux-idmsux)` inline flag construct within the pattern.
**Metadata rules**: FPRM-only `metaRule` parameters allow adding matched regex content as metadata on TextUnits (not accessible via UI).

examples.title: Key-value extraction
examples.description: Extract translatable values from a simple `key=value` or `key:value` format, using the key as the resource identifier.
examples.config:
```
Rule expression: ^\[(.*?)\](=|:)(.*?)$
Action: Extract the content of the source group
Source group: 3
Identifier group: 1
Regex options: Multi-line enabled
```
examples.input:
```
[ID1]=Text for ID1
[ID2]:Text for ID2
```
examples.output:
```xml
<trans-unit id="1" resname="ID1" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en">Text for ID1</source>
</trans-unit>
<trans-unit id="2" resname="ID2" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en">Text for ID2</source>
</trans-unit>
```

examples.title: FPRM metadata rules
examples.description: Add regex-matched content as metadata on TextUnits. This is configured only via FPRM parameters, not the UI.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from Rich Text Format documents.

doc.overview:
RTF (Rich Text Format) is Microsoft's text-based document interchange
format, structured as a stream of control words and groups. It is also the
carrier for Trados-tagged bilingual RTF. neokapi walks the RTF token stream,
extracts text from paragraph runs as translatable Blocks, and skips
non-translatable destinations — font, colour, and stylesheet tables,
document info, pictures, and fields.

neokapi reads and writes RTF. It decodes the common RTF escapes on read —
`\uN` Unicode escapes, `\'HH` hex escapes, and special-character control
words such as `\par`, `\tab`, `\emdash`, and the smart-quote words — and
preserves non-translatable structure as skeleton. By default it does not
extract header and footer text, annotation (comment) text, or bookmark text;
the upstream Okapi RTF filter exposes no parameters, and these neokapi-side
extraction toggles are not surfaced as a configurable JSON schema in this
reference.
limitations: Header/footer, annotation (comment), and bookmark text are not extracted by default.
The neokapi extraction toggles have no upstream Okapi parameter and are not surfaced as configurable schema properties in this reference.
processingNotes: Translatable text comes from paragraph runs; control tables, info, pictures, and fields are skipped.
Decodes `\uN` Unicode escapes, `\'HH` hex escapes, and special-character control words (`\par`, `\tab`, `\emdash`, smart quotes).
Non-translatable destinations are preserved as skeleton for round-trips.

doc.overview: The SDL Trados Package Filter processes SDL Trados SDLPPX (project package) and SDLRPX (return package) files. It extends the Archive Filter, detecting SDLXLIFF files for the specified language pair within the package and processing them using the XLIFF Filter with the `okf_xliff-sdl` configuration. Each SDLXLIFF file inside the package corresponds to a sub-document in the Okapi filter events.

identification.mimeType.help: MIME type the filter reports for the package container. Inherited from the Archive Filter, which this filter extends. Although the published schema shows the Archive base default `application/x-archive`, the SDL package filter overrides it in its constructor to `application/x-sdlpackage`, so that is the effective value unless you set it explicitly. The MIME type identifies the outer container only; the SDLXLIFF sub-documents inside are processed by the XLIFF Filter (`okf_xliff-sdl`) and carry their own XLIFF MIME type.
identification.mimeType.values: MIME type string. Effective default `application/x-sdlpackage` (schema-level default inherited from Archive is `application/x-archive`).

limitations: Reading an SDLPPX file and writing it back produces another SDLPPX file, not an SDLRPX file.
All limitations of the XLIFF Filter also apply, since SDLXLIFF content is processed via the `okf_xliff-sdl` configuration.
processingNotes: Extends the Archive Filter — the package is treated as an archive containing SDLXLIFF sub-documents.
SDLXLIFF files are processed using the XLIFF Filter with the `okf_xliff-sdl` configuration.
Each SDLXLIFF file inside the package maps to a separate sub-document in the filter event stream.
Only SDLXLIFF files matching the specified language pair are detected and processed.

examples.title: Process an SDLPPX project package
examples.description: Open an SDL Trados project package for a French target. The filter resolves the SDLXLIFF files under the target-language folder and processes each with the XLIFF Filter; no MIME type override is needed because the filter sets `application/x-sdlpackage` itself.
examples.config:
```yaml
# No parameters are required for normal use.
# The target language is supplied by the pipeline, not the filter config;
# the filter then looks for <lang>/*.sdlxliff (and one sub-folder level deeper).
```

examples.title: Override the container MIME type
examples.description: Set the MIME type the filter reports for the container. The filter inherits this field from the Archive Filter and defaults it to `application/x-sdlpackage` at construction; override it only when a downstream component needs a different identifier.

entries.description: Read and write plain text where a trailing backslash splices the next line onto the current one.

doc.overview:
Spliced-lines plain text is a line-oriented text format in which a
trailing backslash (`\`) at the end of a physical line continues the
logical line onto the next one. The reader joins adjacent physical
lines wherever the previous line ends in `\`, and emits one
translatable block per logical line. The splice marker is consumed;
the line break between spliced lines is kept inside the block text.

Use it for line-continuation conventions common in configuration and
scripting files, where a long value is wrapped across several
physical lines with a backslash. Lines without a trailing backslash
stand on their own as one block each.
limitations: The splice marker is fixed to the backslash character; it is not configurable.
Blank or whitespace-only logical lines are treated as non-translatable structure (skeleton), not extracted.
processingNotes: One block per logical line; spliced physical lines are joined with their original line breaks preserved in the block text.
A document that ends mid-continuation (a final line still ending in `\`) round-trips the trailing backslash, matching Okapi's `SplicedLinesFilter` behaviour.
With a skeleton store active, line endings and continuation markers are tracked so output is byte-exact.

examples.title: Extract continuation-joined lines
examples.description:
No configuration is required. Declare the format to join
backslash-continued lines into single blocks.

entries.description: Read and write SubRip (.srt) subtitle files, extracting each cue's text for translation.

schema.title: SRT Subtitles
schema.description: SubRip subtitle format — no configurable parameters

doc.overview:
SubRip (`.srt`) is the line-oriented subtitle format used by most
video players. A file is a sequence of cues, each made of a sequence
number, a timecode range (`00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:04,000`), and one
or more lines of subtitle text, separated by a blank line.

The reader extracts each cue's subtitle text as a translatable block
and keeps the sequence number and timecode as block properties so the
writer can reconstruct the cue. Sequence numbers, timecodes, and the
blank-line separators are non-translatable structure: they are
carried in the skeleton and round-trip unchanged. Multi-line cues
preserve their internal line breaks in the block text, so a wrapped
caption stays wrapped.
limitations: Timecodes are preserved verbatim, not adjusted; retiming for a longer or shorter translation is out of scope.
SubRip styling tags inside cue text (for example `…` or `{\an8}`) are carried as part of the block text rather than parsed into inline codes.
processingNotes: One translatable block per cue; the sequence number and timecode ride along as block properties (`sequence`, `timecode`).
With a skeleton store active, sequence numbers, timecodes, and separators are stored as skeleton text for byte-exact round-trips, including the original LF / CRLF line endings.

examples.title: Translate subtitles
examples.description:
SubRip needs no configuration. Declare the format on a collection
to extract every cue's text; the cue numbering and timecodes are
preserved automatically.
