schema.title: CSV Format
schema.description: Configuration for the CSV/TSV format reader/writer

codeFinderRules.title: Code Finder Rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

columnNamesRow.title: Column Names Row
columnNamesRow.description: 1-based row number containing column names. 0 means auto-detect.

commentColumns.title: Comment Columns
commentColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) that contain comments or notes

hasHeader.title: Has Header Row
hasHeader.description: If true, the first row is treated as headers (non-translatable)

keyColumns.title: Key Columns
keyColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) that provide the block ID (source ID / record ID)

separator.title: Field Delimiter
separator.description: Field delimiter character (comma for CSV, tab for TSV)

textQualifier.title: Text Qualifier
textQualifier.description: Character used to quote field values containing delimiters or newlines

translatableColumns.title: Translatable Columns
translatableColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) to extract as translatable content. If empty, all columns are translatable.

trimValues.title: Trim Values
trimValues.description: If true, leading and trailing whitespace is removed from cell values

useCodeFinder.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection within cell values

valuesStartRow.title: Values Start Row
valuesStartRow.description: 1-based row number where data values begin. 0 means auto-detect.

doc.overview:
The **TSV** format reads tab-separated value files, extracts selected
columns as translatable blocks, and writes the translations back into the
same rows. It shares its implementation with the CSV format, configured with
a tab delimiter, so the same column-selection, key, comment, and inline-code
options apply.

By default the first row is treated as a header and every data column is
translatable. You can restrict extraction to specific columns, designate key
columns that supply each block's ID, mark comment columns whose values
become notes, and adjust the delimiter, quote character, and row offsets.

codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within cells.
codeFinderRules.help:
An array of regular expressions; text matching any of them is treated
as an inline code rather than translatable words. Only used when
`useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these patterns to apply

columnNamesRow.help:
The 1-based row that holds the column names. `0` means auto-detect: row
1 is used when `hasHeader` is enabled.

commentColumns.description: 0-based indices of columns holding comments or notes.
commentColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose values are recorded as notes on the
row's translatable blocks rather than extracted as content.

hasHeader.description: Treat the first row as a non-translatable header.
hasHeader.help:
When enabled (the default), the first row is treated as column headers
and is not extracted. Disable it for files whose data begins on the
first row.

keyColumns.description: 0-based indices of columns providing the block ID.
keyColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose values supply each block's ID
(source/record identifier). When several are given, their values are
joined with `.`. When empty, row-based IDs are used.
keyColumns.values: Array of 0-based integers (default empty — row-based IDs)
examples: `[0]` — use the first column as the record ID

separator.help:
The character separating fields — a tab for TSV. The same setting drives
the CSV variant with a comma.
separator.values: Single character (default `,`; use a tab for TSV)

textQualifier.description: Quote character for fields with delimiters or newlines.
textQualifier.help:
The character used to quote field values that contain the delimiter, a
newline, or the quote character itself.

translatableColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose cells are extracted as translatable
content. When empty, every column is treated as translatable.
translatableColumns.values: Array of 0-based integers (default empty — all columns)
examples: `[1, 2]` — extract the second and third columns

trimValues.description: Strip surrounding whitespace from cell values.
trimValues.help:
When enabled, leading and trailing whitespace is removed from each cell
value before extraction.

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline-code detection.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes within cell values. The
patterns are supplied via `codeFinderRules`.

valuesStartRow.help:
The 1-based row from which data values are read. `0` means auto-detect:
the row after the header, or row 1 when there is no header.

limitations: Column selection is index-based; columns are not addressed by header name.
TSV and CSV share one schema; the only intrinsic difference is the tab delimiter.
processingNotes: Each selected cell becomes a translatable block; row structure and the tab delimiter form the non-translatable skeleton.
Key columns supply IDs and comment columns supply notes; they are not themselves extracted as translatable content.

examples.title: Translate one column, keyed by another
examples.description: Extract column 2, using column 0 as the record ID.

examples.title: Headerless file
examples.description: Read a tab-separated file whose data begins on the first row.

schema.title: TTML Filter
schema.description: Timed Text Markup Language (W3C standard) subtitle format

cjkCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line for CJK
cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line for CJK languages. Set to 0 to disable.

escapeBR.title: Escape line breaks
escapeBR.description:
When true,
elements are removed and text is joined with spaces; when false,
is preserved as literal text.

maxCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line
maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line in output. Set to 0 to disable.

maxLinesPerCaption.title: Max lines per caption
maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of lines per caption in output. Set to 0 to disable.

mergeAdjacentCaptions.title: Merge adjacent captions
mergeAdjacentCaptions.description:
Merge adjacent

elements ending with trailing punctuation into one block.

splitWords.title: Split words at character limit
splitWords.description: Split words so that they don't go over the character limit per line.

doc.overview:
TTML (Timed Text Markup Language) is the W3C XML standard for timed text — subtitles and captions — also distributed as `.dfxp`. A TTML document carries its captions inside a ``/`
`/`

` hierarchy in the `http://www.w3.org/ns/ttml` namespace, with timing and positioning expressed as attributes (`begin`, `end`, `dur`, `region`, `textAlign`). The reader walks the `

` hierarchy and emits one translatable block per non-empty `

`. The timing and positioning attributes are kept on the block's properties and written back unchanged. Inline styling such as `` is unwrapped into the block's text; `
` handling is controlled by `escapeBR`. This mirrors Okapi's TTML filter, with the native default of one block per `

`.

cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line for CJK languages.
cjkCharsPerLine.help:
A separate per-line character cap for CJK text, where each character
is visually wider. `0` (the default) falls back to `maxCharsPerLine`.
Output-side; does not affect extraction. Accepted for parity with
Okapi's TTML writer; the native writer does not currently enforce it.

dependsOn.condition: used as the fallback when cjkCharsPerLine is 0

escapeBR.description:
Control whether `
` line breaks are collapsed or kept as literal text.
escapeBR.help:
Governs how `
` elements inside a caption are extracted. When enabled (the default), `
` is removed and the surrounding text is joined with a single space, so the block holds one flowing line of text. When disabled, `
` is preserved as the literal string `
` inside the block's source text, keeping the in-caption line breaks visible to the translator.

maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line of caption text on output.
maxCharsPerLine.help:
Caps the line length of written caption text. `0` (the default)
means no limit. This is an output-side parameter modelled on Okapi's
TTML writer; it does not affect extraction, and the native writer
accepts it for configuration parity without currently rewrapping
caption text.

maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of text lines per caption on output.
maxLinesPerCaption.help:
Caps how many lines a single caption may occupy. `0` (the default)
means no limit. Output-side; does not affect extraction. Accepted
for parity with Okapi's TTML writer; the native writer does not
currently enforce it.

mergeAdjacentCaptions.description: Merge consecutive captions that continue a sentence into one block.
mergeAdjacentCaptions.help:
When enabled, an adjacent `

` caption is merged into the previous block when the previous text ends with a continuation punctuation mark (a comma or semicolon). This produces fewer, sentence-level blocks. When disabled (the default), every non-empty `

` becomes its own block.

splitWords.description: Allow splitting words to keep within the character limit.
splitWords.help:
When enabled, words may be broken across lines to honour the
per-line character cap; when disabled, line breaks fall on word
boundaries only. Takes effect only alongside a non-zero character
limit. Accepted for parity with Okapi's TTML writer; the native
writer does not currently rewrap text.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when a character limit is set

limitations: The output line-wrapping parameters (maxCharsPerLine, maxLinesPerCaption, cjkCharsPerLine, splitWords) are accepted for parity with Okapi but are not yet enforced by the native writer; caption text is written as supplied.
processingNotes:
One block is emitted per non-empty `

`; timing and positioning attributes (begin, end, dur, region, textAlign) are preserved on block properties.
Inline styling such as `<span>` is unwrapped into the block's source text.

examples.title: Keep in-caption line breaks
examples.description:
Preserve `
` as literal text so the translator can see line breaks.

examples.title: Merge sentence-spanning captions
examples.description: Combine adjacent captions that continue a sentence into one block.

cjkCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line for CJK

splitWords.title: Split words so that they don't go over the char limit

doc.overview: The TTML Filter reads and writes **W3C Timed Text Markup Language** caption and subtitle documents (`.ttml`). It extracts the text of each caption as a translatable unit while preserving the timing structure, and applies the shared subtitle output controls when re-emitting captions — caption-text line wrapping, line and character limits, and timecode handling. It builds on the common subtitle filter, so the `output.*` parameters are shared with the WebVTT filter.

output.cjkCharsPerLine.help: A separate per-line character limit applied when the target locale is Chinese, Japanese, or Korean, where each character occupies more visual width. When the target is CJK, this value is used in place of `maxCharsPerLine`.
output.cjkCharsPerLine.values: Integer `1`–9999999, or `0` for no limit. Parameter default `0`; the bundled `okf_ttml` configuration sets `18`.

dependsOn.condition: used instead of maxCharsPerLine when the target locale is CJK

output.keepTimecodes.help: Controls how timecodes are written when a caption was assembled from several timed source captions. When enabled, each original begin/end pair is written back verbatim. When disabled (the default), the combined caption's overall duration is divided evenly across its parts and re-emitted as recomputed timecodes.

output.maxCharsPerLine.help: The maximum number of characters per line of caption text on output. When greater than `0`, written caption text is wrapped to honour this limit; `0` means no limit. For CJK target locales, `cjkCharsPerLine` is used instead of this value.
output.maxCharsPerLine.values: Integer `1`–9999999, or `0` for no limit. Parameter default `0`; the bundled `okf_ttml` configuration sets `47`.

dependsOn.condition: cjkCharsPerLine replaces this value for CJK target locales

output.maxLinesPerCaption.help: The maximum number of text lines a single caption may occupy on output. When greater than `0`, caption text is distributed across at most this many lines; `0` means no limit.
output.maxLinesPerCaption.values: Integer `1`–9999999, or `0` for no limit. Parameter default `0`; the bundled `okf_ttml` configuration sets `2`.

output.splitWords.help: Determines how the per-line character limit is enforced. When enabled (the default), words may be broken across lines so a line never exceeds the character limit. When disabled, line breaks fall only on word boundaries, even if that leaves a line shorter than the limit. Has effect only when a character limit is set.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when a per-line character limit is in effect

output.timeFormat.help: The pattern used to format begin/end times on output, following Java date-time formatting. It is applied whenever the writer emits a timecode.
output.timeFormat.values: A Java time-format pattern string. Parameter default `HH:mm:ss.SSS` (hours, minutes, seconds, milliseconds).
examples: `HH:mm:ss,SSS` — comma decimal separator, as used by SubRip-style timing

processingNotes: The default `okf_ttml` configuration ships with non-zero line limits (`maxLinesPerCaption` 2, `maxCharsPerLine` 47, `cjkCharsPerLine` 18) suited to broadcast captions; the underlying parameter defaults when set programmatically are `0` (no limit).

When a character/line limit is in effect, caption text is re-wrapped on output. The character limit chosen is `cjkCharsPerLine` for CJK target locales (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) and `maxCharsPerLine` otherwise.
Output time strings are formatted with the `output.timeFormat` pattern; the parameter default is `HH:mm:ss.SSS`.

The filter enables its inline code finder by default and, separately, controls `
` handling via an `escapeBrMode` parameter (not part of the documented output set here).

examples.title: Constrain caption layout for broadcast
examples.description: Limits captions to two lines of at most 37 characters and breaks only on word boundaries, so written captions stay within a typical on-screen budget.
examples.input:
```xml

A single long line of caption text that exceeds the limit.

```
examples.output:
```
The caption text is wrapped to at most two lines of 37 characters, breaking at spaces rather than mid-word.
```

examples.title: Preserve original timecodes
examples.description: Keeps each caption's original begin/end times verbatim instead of redistributing time across merged sub-captions.

options.label: Auto-detect existing segments (If found: extract only those, otherwise extract all)
Extract all (existing segments and un-segmented text parts)

escapeGT.title: Escape Greater-Than Characters
escapeGT.description: Escape all > characters as > in the output.

doc.overview: The TTX Filter processes **Trados TTX documents**, an XML-based bilingual format used by some versions of Trados tools and supported by several other translation tools. There are no official public specifications for TTX. The filter handles extraction of source and target text, respects existing segmentation, and carries over match percentages and translation annotations.

escapeGT.description: Escape all > characters as > in the output.
escapeGT.help: Escapes all `>` characters as `>` in the output document. By default, XML does not require `>` to be escaped, but some downstream tools may expect it.

mode.segmentMode.help:
Integer selector for how existing TTX segments affect extraction:

- `0` — **Auto-detect**: scans the file for existing `<Tu>` segments; if at least one is found, only segmented text is extracted, otherwise all text is extracted.
- `1` — **Extract only existing segments**: skip all non-segmented text (nothing is extracted from a file with no pre-segmentation).
- `2` — **Extract all**: extract every text part, wrapping unsegmented text into new segments.

In extract-all mode, contiguous text across segment boundaries is merged into a single text unit with multiple segments.
mode.segmentMode.values: Integer enum: `0` (auto-detect, default), `1` (existing segments only), `2` (extract all).
notes: For better interoperability with the tool that created the TTX files, work with pre-segmented documents and use mode 0 or 1.

limitations: The TTX `<df>` element may cause problems when it spans across external tag boundaries (e.g., bold formatting spanning across paragraph boundaries in extracted HTML).
No official public specifications exist for the TTX format.
processingNotes: Input encoding is determined from the document's encoding declaration; falls back to **UTF-8** if none is present.
Output BOM handling: if input was UTF-8 with a BOM, the output preserves the BOM; if input was not UTF-8, no BOM is written.
Line-break style from the original input is preserved in the output.

Segmented entries with translations extract both source and target text. Match types are mapped: `MatchPercent > 100` with `Origin=xtranslate` → `EXACT_LOCAL_CONTEXT` (XU matches in TagEditor), `> 99` → `EXACT`, `1–99` → `FUZZY`.
The `Origin` attribute from translated segments is carried over to translation annotations.

examples.title: Extract-all mode (mode 2) vs existing-segments-only (mode 1)
examples.description: Demonstrates how extraction mode affects which text is extracted from a TTX file containing mixed segmented and unsegmented content.
examples.input:
```xml
<Raw>
Part 1 <Tu MatchPercent="0">
<Tuv Lang="EN">Part 2</Tuv></Tu> Part 3.
</Raw>
```
examples.output:
```
Mode 2 (extract all): [Part 1 ][Part 2][ Part 3]  — single text unit, three segments
Mode 1 (existing only): [Part 2]  — only the pre-segmented part
```

allowEmptyOutputTarget.title: Allow Empty Target Segments
allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: Allow empty target in output when no translation.

doc.overview: The TXML Filter processes **Wordfast Pro TXML documents**, a proprietary XML-based bilingual format used by Wordfast Pro and supported by several other tools. It extracts blocks of one or more segments as text units, preserving existing translations and handling revisions. There are no official public specifications for the TXML format.

allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: Allow empty translations to be written as-is in output; when disabled, source text is copied into empty targets.
allowEmptyOutputTarget.help:
Controls what happens when a translated segment has an empty target:

- **Enabled**: Empty translations are written as-is to the output TXML file
- **Disabled**: A copy of the source text is used in place of the empty translation

This is useful when downstream tools require non-empty target segments, or when you want to preserve empty targets as a signal that translation is still needed.
notes: When disabled, empty targets are replaced with a copy of the source — this modifies the output without affecting the internal translation memory or segment status.

limitations: The TXML format does not allow different source and target content for inter-segment parts. If these parts change after extraction, only the source representation is preserved on merge — the difference cannot be represented.
processingNotes: **Encoding detection**: Uses the XML encoding declaration if present; otherwise defaults to UTF-8 regardless of any externally specified default encoding.
**BOM handling**: When output is UTF-8 and input was also UTF-8, a BOM is written only if one was detected in the input. If converting from another encoding to UTF-8, no BOM is added.
**XML declaration**: If the original document had an XML encoding declaration it is updated on output; if it did not have one, a declaration is automatically added.
**Line-breaks**: Output line-break style matches the original input document.
**Segmentation**: TXML files are always segmented — each block contains one or more segments. The content of each block is extracted as a single text unit with all its segments.
**Existing translations**: Translated segments are extracted with both source and target. Segments marked `gtmt="true"` (without `modified="true"`) also generate an `AltTranslationsAnnotation`.
**Revisions**: Only the latest translation is extracted; `<revisions>` elements are ignored. On merge, updated translations overwrite originals — no revision entries are created in the output.

examples.title: Segment with machine translation annotation
examples.description: When a TXML segment has `gtmt="true"` (machine-translated) but not `modified="true"`, the filter extracts both source and target and creates an `AltTranslationsAnnotation`. This allows downstream steps to distinguish machine-translated content from human-reviewed translations.
examples.input:
```xml
<segment gtmt="true">
  <source>Hello world</source>
  <target>Bonjour le monde</target>
</segment>
```

entries.description: Read and write verse-numbered plain text (scripture, poetry, lyrics), one block per verse.

doc.overview:
Versified text is a line-oriented format for verse-numbered content
such as scripture, poetry, and lyrics. Each line opens with a verse
marker — either USFM-style (`\v 1` or `\v1`) or a plain numeric
prefix (`1.` or `1 `). The marker is consumed and the rest of the
line becomes one translatable block; the verse number is kept in the
block's `verse` property so the writer can re-emit the original
numbering.

Lines with no recognised marker are still extracted as blocks (with
an empty `verse` property), so non-numbered material is not lost.
Blank lines act as stanza breaks: they are preserved as
non-translatable structure and never translated.
limitations: Recognised markers are USFM `\v` (with or without a space) and a leading positive integer followed by `.` or a space; other numbering schemes are treated as ordinary lines.
This is a native-only format; the okapi-bridge daemon ships no equivalent filter.
processingNotes: One block per line; the verse number rides along in the block's `verse` property and the block name follows `verse.<N>`.
Blank lines between verses emit a stanza-break Data part and round-trip as skeleton.
With a skeleton store active, the verse marker prefix and line endings are stored as skeleton text for byte-exact round-trips.

examples.title: Translate verse-numbered text
examples.description:
No configuration is required. Declare the format to extract one
block per verse while preserving verse numbers and stanza breaks.

forceTargetOutput.title: Force Target Output
forceTargetOutput.description: Always produce target output even when no translation is available.

doc.overview: The Plain Text filter processes text files encoded in ANSI, Unicode, UTF-8, and UTF-16 with automatic Byte-Order-Mark detection. It provides three extraction modes: **line-based** (`okf_baseplaintext`), **paragraph-based** (`okf_paraplaintext`), and **regex-based** (`okf_regexplaintext`), plus a **spliced lines** variant (`okf_splicedlines`) for source code with line-continuation characters. Each mode shares common parser and inline code settings but exposes different extraction parameters.

extraction.createPlaceholders.help: Creates an inline code placeholder at each position where a splicer character was removed during line merging. This preserves the original line break positions in the translatable content, allowing translators to see where breaks occurred.
notes: Only active in the spliced lines sub-filter (`okf_splicedlines`).

extraction.extractParagraphs.help:
Groups consecutive non-empty lines into paragraph text units, separated by one or more blank lines. Each paragraph becomes a single translatable unit with internal line breaks handled according to `wrapMode`.

When disabled, each line becomes its own text unit.
notes: Only available in the paragraph-based sub-filter (`okf_paraplaintext`).

extraction.regexOptions.help:
Bitmask of `java.util.regex.Pattern` flags to apply to the extraction regex. Common flags:

- **8** = `MULTILINE` — `^` and `$` match line boundaries (not just start/end of input)
- **32** = `DOTALL` — `.` matches any character including newline
- **40** = `MULTILINE | DOTALL` (8 + 32)
extraction.regexOptions.values: Integer bitmask combining `java.util.regex.Pattern` flag constants: `CASE_INSENSITIVE` (2), `MULTILINE` (8), `DOTALL` (32), etc.
examples: `8` — MULTILINE: for line-by-line extraction
`40` — MULTILINE + DOTALL: for paragraph extraction across line boundaries

extraction.rule.help:
Java regular expression used to extract translatable text. The filter applies this regex to the input and creates text units from the matches. Use capturing groups to isolate the translatable portion, then set `sourceGroup` to the group index.

The regex is applied with the flags specified in `regexOptions`.
extraction.rule.values: Java `java.util.regex.Pattern` syntax. Use capturing groups `()` to isolate translatable text.
examples: `(^(?=.+))(.*?)$` — extract each non-empty line (group 2 = content, with MULTILINE flag)

`(
*)(.*?)(

|\Z)` — extract paragraphs separated by blank lines (group 2 = paragraph text, with MULTILINE + DOTALL flags)

extraction.sample.help: Sample text used to test the extraction regex interactively. Extracted matches are shown enclosed in square brackets below the sample. This is for preview/testing only and does not affect processing.

extraction.sourceGroup.help:
The 1-based index of the capturing group in the extraction regex whose match contains the translatable text. Group 0 is the entire match.

For example, with regex `(prefix)(content)(suffix)`, set to `2` to extract only the `content` group.
extraction.sourceGroup.values: Integer index of a capturing group defined in the extraction `rule`. Must be ≥ 0.

extraction.splicer.help:
The character that indicates a line continues on the next line (line-continuation character). When found at the end of a line, the filter merges that line with the following one into a single text unit.

Common splicers: `\` (backslash, used in C/C++/shell), `_` (underscore, used in VB).
extraction.splicer.values: A single character. Common values: `\` (backslash), `_` (underscore), or any custom character.
notes: Only active in the spliced lines sub-filter (`okf_splicedlines`).
examples: `\` — backslash splicer for C/shell continuation lines
`_` — underscore splicer for Visual Basic continuation lines

inlineCodes.enabled.help: Activates regex-based detection of inline codes within extracted text. Any text matching the defined patterns will be converted to inline code placeholders, protecting them from translation.

inlineCodes.rules.help:
Define regex patterns to identify inline codes (placeholders, format specifiers, escape sequences, etc.) within translatable text. Each pattern match is converted to an inline code placeholder.

Default rules typically match C-style format specifiers (`%d`, `%s`) and escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`).
examples: `(\\r\\n)|\\a|\\b|\\f|\\n|\\r|\\t|\\v` — C escape sequences

parser.preserveWS.help: Preserves original whitespace in extracted text units. Typically enabled for paragraph and regex modes where whitespace structure matters.

parser.trimLeading.help: Strips leading spaces and tabs from each extracted text unit. Useful for indented source files where leading whitespace is not part of the translatable content.
notes: For CSV-type usage, this works together with the CSV "Exclude leading/trailing white spaces" option.

parser.trimTrailing.help: Strips trailing spaces and tabs from each extracted text unit. Commonly used to clean up lines with trailing whitespace before translation.
notes: For CSV-type usage, this works together with the CSV "Exclude leading/trailing white spaces" option.

parser.unescapeSource.help: Converts escape sequences (`\t`, `\n`, `\\`, `\uXXXX`) in the source text into their corresponding characters before extraction. Disable if the file contains literal backslash sequences that should be preserved as-is.

parser.wrapMode.help:
Controls how multiple lines are combined into a single text unit when extracting multi-line content:

- **NONE** — Lines are separated by `\n` line feeds (preserves original line structure)
- **SPACES** — Lines are unwrapped and merged with a space between them (useful for reflowable text)
- **PLACEHOLDERS** — Lines are joined with an inline code placeholder representing the original line break (allows translators to reposition breaks)
parser.wrapMode.values: `NONE` = separate with line feeds, `SPACES` = unwrap/merge with spaces, `PLACEHOLDERS` = inline code placeholders for line breaks

processingNotes: The filter auto-detects Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) to determine encoding. If no BOM is found, the configured default encoding is used.
For UTF-8 output: a BOM is written only if the input was UTF-8 *and* had a BOM. Converting from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 produces no BOM.
Output line-break type preserves the original input's line-break convention (CR, LF, or CRLF).
The regex-based sub-filter (`okf_regexplaintext`) detects a wider range of linebreaks at the cost of lower speed and higher memory usage compared to the line-based sub-filter.

examples.title: Line-by-line extraction with trailing trim
examples.description: Uses the base plain text filter to extract each line as a separate text unit, trimming trailing whitespace.

examples.title: Paragraph extraction
examples.description: Groups consecutive non-empty lines into paragraph text units. Paragraphs are separated by one or more blank lines.
examples.input:
```text
This is the first sentence.
And this continues the paragraph.

This is a new paragraph.
```
examples.output:
```text
Text Unit 1: "This is the first sentence.\nAnd this continues the paragraph."
Text Unit 2: "This is a new paragraph."
```

examples.title: Regex extraction by line
examples.description: Uses a regex rule with MULTILINE flag to extract each non-empty line. Group 2 captures the line content.

examples.title: Spliced lines with backslash continuation
examples.description: Merges lines ending with backslash into single text units, inserting inline code placeholders where splicers were removed.
examples.input:
```text
This line continues \
on the next line.
This is separate.
```
examples.output:
```text
Text Unit 1: "This line continues <x/>on the next line."
Text Unit 2: "This is separate."
```

entries.description: Read and write Vignette CMS export/import XML (the output of the vgnexport tool).

doc.overview:
This format reads and writes the export/import XML produced by the Vignette content management system's `vgnexport` tool. A document contains one or more `` blocks, each holding a set of `` payloads for one content item in one locale. The reader walks every instance and extracts the payloads whose attribute names are listed in the parts configuration; the remaining XML structure is preserved. By default the reader works bilingually: source and target instances are paired through the value of the source-id attribute, and only paired instances reach the translatable stream. In monolingual mode every instance is extracted independently. Encoding is forced to UTF-8 on read and write, matching the upstream filter. A payload may itself contain HTML; for attributes configured with the `okf_html` sub-filter the reader decodes HTML entities and strips an outer `

` wrapper, while every other configuration treats the payload as a single literal block. Configuration is supplied on the collection's `format:` block in a `.kapi` project, with keys that mirror the Okapi parameter names: `partsNames` — a comma-separated list of attribute names to extract (defaults to the standard SMC attribute set); `partsConfigurations` — a comma-separated list of sub-filter ids paired by index with `partsNames`, where `okf_html` decodes HTML and any other value (including `default`) means no sub-filtering; `sourceId` — the attribute name holding the source-id used to pair instances (default `SOURCE_ID`); `localeId` — the attribute name holding the locale identifier (default `LOCALE_ID`); `monolingual` — when true, extract every instance independently instead of pairing; and `useCDATA` — when true, wrap written payloads in a CDATA section (write-side only).
limitations: In bilingual mode (the default), only instances that pair through the source-id attribute are extracted; unpaired instances are skipped.
The parts configuration recognises only `okf_html` as a sub-filter; every other value, including `default`, is treated as no sub-filtering.
Input and output encoding are forced to UTF-8.
processingNotes: One block per extracted attribute payload; `partsNames` and `partsConfigurations` are paired by list index.
Source and target instances pair via the `sourceId` attribute value unless `monolingual` is enabled.
The `useCDATA` option affects only the writer; it wraps emitted payloads in a CDATA section and has no effect on reading.

examples.title: Extract specific attributes monolingually
examples.description:
Override the parts list and extract every instance independently,
decoding the body attribute as HTML.
examples.config:
format:
  name: vignette
  config:
    partsNames: "SMCCONTENT-TITLE, SMCCONTENT-BODY"
    partsConfigurations: "default, okf_html"
    monolingual: true

partsNames.description: Comma-separated list of the names of the <attribute> elements to extract.

doc.overview: The Vignette Filter processes export/import XML documents used with the **Vignette Content Management System**. It handles files generated by the `vgnexport` tool of Vignette. Input and output encodings are forced to **UTF-8**. The filter extracts entries based on the `name` attribute of `<attribute>` elements and supports both bilingual (source+target matching via source ID) and monolingual modes.

localeId.description: Name attribute value of the element containing the locale identifier for a content block.
localeId.help: The `name` attribute value of the `<attribute>` element that contains the **locale identifier** for a given content block.
localeId.values: A string matching the `name` attribute in the Vignette export XML (e.g., `MYVCM-LOCALE`)

mode.quoteMode.help: Selects how quote characters are escaped in written output, consulted only when `quoteModeDefined` is set. The Vignette filter always forces the value `0` (`UNESCAPED`), so `"` and `'` in extracted element text are written literally rather than as `"`/`'`. This is safe because every part the filter extracts comes from element content, not from attribute values where unescaped quotes would be a problem.
mode.quoteMode.values: Integer quote-mode code; filter forces `0` (UNESCAPED) on write
notes: Other XMLEncoder quote modes (1 ALL, 2 numeric single quotes, 3 double quotes only) are not used by this filter.

dependsOn.condition: consulted only when quoteModeDefined is true

mode.quoteModeDefined.help: Activates an explicit quote-escaping policy for written output via the `quoteMode` value. The Vignette filter always forces this on internally — it is a write-only flag the filter sets so the XML encoder knows it has been given a definite `quoteMode` rather than the encoder's own default. There is no reason to change it in normal use; it exists in the schema only to surface the filter's fixed write-side behaviour.
notes: Not an interactive option in the Okapi UI; the filter writes it unconditionally.

monolingual.description: Process all importContentInstance elements without requiring source/target pairing.
monolingual.help:
Extracts **all** `importContentInstance` elements regardless of source/target pairing. The source ID and locale ID parameters are ignored when this is enabled.

Use this for documents that contain only one language rather than source+target pairs.

dependsOn.condition: ignored when monolingual mode is enabled
ignored when monolingual mode is enabled

partsConfigurations.description: Comma-separated list of filter configurations corresponding to each extractable element.
partsConfigurations.help:
Comma-separated list of filter configurations to apply to each extracted part. Must contain the **same number of entries** as the parts-names list, in the **same order**.

Use `default` to indicate no specific sub-filter for a given entry.
partsConfigurations.values: Comma-separated filter configuration IDs. Use `default` for no specific filter.
notes: The number of entries **must match** the number of parts names exactly.
examples: `default` — use no specific filter for a single entry
`okf_html,default` — apply HTML filter to first entry, no filter to second

dependsOn.condition: must have the same number of comma-separated entries, in matching order

partsNames.description: Comma-separated list of name attribute values for the <attribute> elements to extract.
partsNames.help:
Comma-separated list of `name` attribute values identifying which `<attribute>` elements to extract from the Vignette export XML.

For example, if content is stored in `MYVCM-BODY`:
```xml
<attribute name="MYVCM-BODY">
  <valueCLOB>data</valueCLOB>
</attribute>
```
then include `MYVCM-BODY` in this list.
partsNames.values: Comma-separated string of attribute name values (e.g., `MYVCM-BODY,MYVCM-TITLE`)
examples: `MYVCM-BODY` — extract the main content body
`MYVCM-BODY,MYVCM-TITLE` — extract both body and title entries

sourceId.description: Name attribute value of the element containing the source ID that pairs source and target blocks.
sourceId.help:
The `name` attribute value of the `<attribute>` element that holds the **source ID** linking source and target `importContentInstance` blocks together.

Both the source and target blocks must share the same source ID value for the filter to pair them correctly.
sourceId.values: A string matching the `name` attribute in the Vignette export XML (e.g., `MYVCM-SOURCEID`)
notes: Entries with only a source `importContentInstance` (no matching target) are **not extracted** but generate a warning.
Entries with no source `importContentInstance` at all are also skipped with a warning.

limitations: Very large documents may cause memory problems.
Both source and target `importContentInstance` elements must be present for extraction in bilingual mode — missing either side produces a warning and skips the entry.
processingNotes: Input and output encodings are **forced to UTF-8** regardless of any encoding settings.
Entries to extract are identified by the `name` attribute of `<attribute>` elements in the Vignette XML export.
Source/target pairing is done via a shared source ID value across `importContentInstance` blocks.
Monolingual mode bypasses the source/target pairing constraints entirely.

examples.title: Vignette export XML structure
examples.description: Shows the XML structure the filter processes, with extractable `<attribute>` elements inside `importContentInstance` blocks.
examples.input:
```xml
<attribute name="MYVCM-BODY">
  <valueCLOB>data</valueCLOB>
</attribute>
```

examples.title: Extracting multiple attributes with sub-filters
examples.description: Configure the filter to extract two attributes, applying an HTML filter to the body and no filter to the title.
examples.config:
```
Elements to extract: MYVCM-BODY,MYVCM-TITLE
Filter configurations: okf_html,default
```

okf_vignette-nocdata.partsConfigurations: default, okf_html, okf_html, default, default, okf_html, default, default, okf_html, default, okf_html, okf_html, default
okf_vignette-nocdata.partsNames: SMCCONTENT-TITLE, SMCCONTENT-ABSTRACT, SMCCONTENT-BODY, SMCCONTENT-ALT, SMCCHANNELDESCRIPTOR-TITLE, SMCCHANNELDESCRIPTOR-ABSTRACT, SMCCHANNELDESCRIPTOR-ALT, SMCLINKCOLLECTIONS-LINKCOLLECTION-TITLE, SMCLINKCOLLECTIONS-LINKCOLLECTION-DESCRIPTION, SMCLINKS-TITLE, SMCLINKS-ABSTRACT, SMCLINKS-BODY, SMCLINKS-ALT

cueSettings.title: Replace existing cue settings with text

cjkCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line for CJK

splitWords.title: Split words so that they don't go over the char limit

doc.overview: The VTT Filter reads and writes **WebVTT** (Web Video Text Tracks) subtitle and caption files (`.vtt`, and `.srt` under the default configuration). Each cue's text is extracted as a translatable unit, while the `WEBVTT` header, cue identifiers, and timecode lines are preserved as structure. On output the filter can rewrite or discard each cue's positioning settings and apply the shared subtitle line-wrapping and timecode controls. It builds on the common subtitle filter, so the `output.*` parameters are shared with the TTML filter.

extraction.cueSettings.help: When set to a non-empty string, the given text replaces the positioning settings of every cue on output. It takes precedence over `discardCues`. Leave empty to preserve each cue's original settings (or to let `discardCues` remove them).
extraction.cueSettings.values: A WebVTT cue-settings string, or empty (default). Empty means the original settings are kept unless `discardCues` is enabled.
examples: `align:center line:90%`
`position:50% size:80%`

dependsOn.condition: a non-empty cueSettings overrides discardCues

extraction.discardCues.help: When enabled, each cue's positioning settings are removed on output, leaving only the timecode. It has no effect if `cueSettings` is set to a non-empty replacement string, which takes precedence. When disabled (the default), the original cue settings are preserved.

dependsOn.condition: ignored when cueSettings provides a non-empty replacement

output.cjkCharsPerLine.help: A separate per-line character limit applied when the target locale is Chinese, Japanese, or Korean, where each character occupies more visual width. When the target is CJK, this value is used in place of `maxCharsPerLine`.
output.cjkCharsPerLine.values: Integer `1`–9999999, or `0` for no limit. Parameter default `0`; the bundled `okf_vtt` configuration sets `18`.

dependsOn.condition: used instead of maxCharsPerLine when the target locale is CJK

output.keepTimecodes.help: Controls how timecodes are written when a cue was assembled from several timed source captions. When enabled, each original begin/end pair is written back verbatim. When disabled (the default), the combined cue's overall duration is divided evenly across its parts and re-emitted as recomputed timecodes.

output.maxCharsPerLine.help: The maximum number of characters per line of cue text on output. When greater than `0`, written cue text is wrapped to honour this limit; `0` means no limit. For CJK target locales, `cjkCharsPerLine` is used instead of this value.
output.maxCharsPerLine.values: Integer `1`–9999999, or `0` for no limit. Parameter default `0`; the bundled `okf_vtt` configuration sets `47`.

dependsOn.condition: cjkCharsPerLine replaces this value for CJK target locales

output.maxLinesPerCaption.help: The maximum number of text lines a single cue may occupy on output. When greater than `0`, cue text is distributed across at most this many lines; `0` means no limit.
output.maxLinesPerCaption.values: Integer `1`–9999999, or `0` for no limit. Parameter default `0`; the bundled `okf_vtt` configuration sets `2`.

output.splitWords.help: Determines how the per-line character limit is enforced. When enabled (the default), words may be broken across lines so a line never exceeds the character limit. When disabled, line breaks fall only on word boundaries, even if that leaves a line shorter than the limit. Has effect only when a character limit is set.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when a per-line character limit is in effect

output.timeFormat.help: The pattern used to format begin/end times on output, following Java date-time formatting. It is applied whenever the writer emits a timecode line.
output.timeFormat.values: A Java time-format pattern string. Parameter default `HH:mm:ss.SSS` (hours, minutes, seconds, milliseconds).
examples: `HH:mm:ss.SSS` — `01:23:45.678` (WebVTT uses a dot decimal separator)

processingNotes: A WebVTT cue is `[identifier]` + timecode line (`HH:MM:SS.mmm --> HH:MM:SS.mmm` plus optional positioning settings) + one or more lines of cue text. The cue text is the translatable unit.

Cue settings (the positioning tokens after the timecode, e.g. `align:start position:10%`) are governed on output by `extraction.cueSettings` and `extraction.discardCues`: a non-empty `cueSettings` value replaces them; otherwise `discardCues` removes them; otherwise the original settings are kept.

The default `okf_vtt` configuration ships with non-zero line limits (`maxLinesPerCaption` 2, `maxCharsPerLine` 47, `cjkCharsPerLine` 18); the underlying parameter defaults when set programmatically are `0` (no limit).
Output time strings are formatted with the `output.timeFormat` pattern; the parameter default is `HH:mm:ss.SSS`.

examples.title: Normalise cue positioning
examples.description: Replaces every cue's positioning settings with a single fixed alignment, useful when consolidating subtitles authored with inconsistent positioning.
examples.input:
```
WEBVTT

00:00:01.000 --> 00:00:04.000 align:start position:10%
Hello there.
```
examples.output:
```
On output the cue timecode is followed by "align:center line:90%" in place of the original "align:start position:10%".
```

examples.title: Strip cue settings and cap line length
examples.description: Discards all positioning settings and constrains caption text to two lines of at most 37 characters.
examples.config:
```yaml
extraction:
  discardCues: true
output:
  maxLinesPerCaption: 2
  maxCharsPerLine: 37
```

entries.description: Read and write WebVTT subtitle files, extracting one block per cue.

schema.title: VTT Filter
schema.description: Web Video Text Tracks (WebVTT) subtitle format

cjkCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line for CJK
cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line for CJK languages. Set to 0 to disable.

maxCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line
maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line in output. Set to 0 to disable.

maxLinesPerCaption.title: Max lines per caption
maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of lines per caption in output. Set to 0 to disable.

splitWords.title: Split words at character limit
splitWords.description: Split words so that they don't go over the character limit per line.

doc.overview:
WebVTT (Web Video Text Tracks) is the W3C subtitle and caption format
used by HTML5 `<track>` elements. A `.vtt` file opens with a `WEBVTT`
header and then lists cues: an optional cue identifier, a timecode line
(`HH:MM:SS.mmm --> HH:MM:SS.mmm` plus optional positioning settings),
and one or more lines of cue text.

The reader emits the `WEBVTT` header as a non-translatable data part,
each cue identifier as its own data part, and one translatable block per
cue. The timecode (including any cue settings) is preserved on the
block's `timecode` property and written back verbatim, so the timing
survives the round-trip. Cues are kept separate — captions are not
merged by sentence — so each cue is translated independently.

cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line for CJK languages.
cjkCharsPerLine.help:
A separate per-line character cap applied to CJK text, where each
character is visually wider. `0` (the default) falls back to
`maxCharsPerLine`. Accepted for parity with Okapi's WebVTT writer;
the native writer does not currently enforce it.

dependsOn.condition: used as the fallback when cjkCharsPerLine is 0

maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line of cue text on output.
maxCharsPerLine.help:
Caps the line length of written cue text. `0` (the default) means no
limit. This is an output-side parameter modelled on Okapi's WebVTT
writer; the native writer accepts it for configuration parity but
does not currently rewrap cue text — lines are written as supplied
by the block.

maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of text lines per cue on output.
maxLinesPerCaption.help:
Caps how many lines a single cue may occupy. `0` (the default) means
no limit. Accepted for parity with Okapi's WebVTT writer; the native
writer does not currently enforce it.

splitWords.description: Allow splitting words to keep within the character limit.
splitWords.help:
When enabled, words may be broken across lines to honour the
per-line character cap; when disabled, line breaks fall on word
boundaries only. Takes effect only alongside a non-zero character
limit. Accepted for parity with Okapi's WebVTT writer; the native
writer does not currently rewrap text.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when a character limit is set

limitations: The output line-wrapping parameters (maxCharsPerLine, maxLinesPerCaption, cjkCharsPerLine, splitWords) are accepted for parity with Okapi but are not yet enforced by the native writer; cue text is written as supplied.
Cues are never merged by sentence; each cue is a separate block.
processingNotes: The WEBVTT header is emitted as a data part; cue identifiers become their own data parts.
The cue timecode and its settings are preserved on the block `timecode` property and written back unchanged.

examples.title: Default extraction
examples.description: Read each cue as a translatable block with no output line limits.

entries.description: Extract and reinsert translatable text from wiki markup (DokuWiki or MediaWiki).

schema.title: Wiki Filter
schema.description: Configuration for the Wiki (MediaWiki/DokuWiki) format reader/writer

preserveWhitespace.title: Preserve Whitespace
preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in wiki markup instead of normalizing it.

variant.title: Wiki variant
variant.description: Wiki markup variant: mediawiki or dokuwiki. Default is dokuwiki to match the okf_wiki bridge contract.

doc.overview:
The **Wiki** format reads wiki markup, extracts prose as translatable
blocks, and writes the translations back while preserving the markup
structure — headings, lists, tables, links, and formatting. Inline markup is
exposed as inline codes so the surrounding syntax survives translation.

Two markup dialects are supported: DokuWiki and MediaWiki. The default is
DokuWiki, matching the upstream `okf_wiki` filter, whose documentation states
DokuWiki is the supported markup style; set `variant` to `mediawiki` to read
MediaWiki markup instead.

preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in the markup.
preserveWhitespace.help:
When enabled, original whitespace in the wiki markup is preserved rather
than normalized during extraction. By default whitespace is normalized.

limitations: The two dialects share one extraction engine; markup specific to one dialect is recognized only under the matching `variant`.
processingNotes: Headings, lists, tables, and formatting markers form the non-translatable skeleton; prose runs become translatable blocks.
Inline formatting and links within a run are exposed as inline codes so they survive translation.

examples.title: Read MediaWiki markup
examples.description: Parse a MediaWiki-flavoured document instead of the default DokuWiki.

examples.title: Preserve whitespace
examples.description: Keep original spacing in the markup instead of normalizing it.

simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation
simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules

path.title: Wiki Path Rules
path.description: Path expressions controlling which wiki elements to extract for translation.

preserve_whitespace.title: Preserve Whitespace
preserve_whitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in wiki markup instead of normalizing it.

doc.overview: The Wiki Filter extracts translatable text from wiki markup. Currently the only supported markup style is [Dokuwiki](https://www.dokuwiki.org/dokuwiki). It extracts text from headers, paragraphs, list items, image captions, and table cells/headers. All Dokuwiki syntax described in the [Dokuwiki syntax reference](https://www.dokuwiki.org/syntax) is supported for inline codes.

customCodes.description: Defines custom regex patterns to be treated as inline codes during extraction.
customCodes.help:
Defines custom inline codes beyond the built-in Dokuwiki syntax. Each entry can be either:

- A **placeholder** (self-closing code) using `pattern` — a single regex matching the entire inline element
- An **opening/closing pair** using `start_pattern` and `end_pattern` — two regexes matching the start and end tags respectively

Matches are converted into inline codes within translation units.
customCodes.values:
Array of objects. Each object has either:
- `pattern`: a regex string for placeholder codes
- `start_pattern` + `end_pattern`: two regex strings for paired codes

Regex patterns must match non-zero-width runs of text.
examples: Placeholder code: `{pattern: "\\[(path|menu)[^\\]]*\\]"}`
Paired code: `{start_pattern: "<custom>", end_pattern: "</custom>"}`

preserveWhitespace.description: Prevents collapsing of consecutive whitespace characters in extracted text.
preserveWhitespace.help: Prevents the filter from collapsing whitespace in extracted text. By default, consecutive whitespace characters are collapsed into a single space. Set to `true` to preserve the original whitespace as-is.
examples: `preserve_whitespace: true` — keeps all original spacing intact

limitations: Attributes of inline codes (link and image URLs, etc.) are not exposed for translation or special processing.
Embedded HTML, PHP, etc., is not extracted.
processingNotes: If the file has a Unicode Byte-Order-Mark, the corresponding encoding (UTF-8, UTF-16, etc.) is used automatically.
If no BOM is present, the default encoding specified when opening the document is used.
All standard Dokuwiki syntax is recognized for inline codes.

examples.title: Custom inline codes configuration
examples.description: Defines a placeholder code for `[path:...]` and `[menu:...]` markup, plus a paired code for custom tags.
examples.config:
```yaml
preserve_whitespace: false
custom_codes:
  - pattern: "\\[(path|menu)[^\\]]*\\]"
  - start_pattern: "<custom>"
    end_pattern: "</custom>"
```
examples.input:
```
=== Page Title ===
Click [menu:File > Save] to save.

^ Header 1 ^ Header 2 |
| Cell 1   | Cell 2   |
```

inlineCodes.title: Inline Codes
inlineCodes.description: Pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, variables, tags) within translatable text.

enabled.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
enabled.description: Enable pattern-based detection of inline codes (placeholders, tags, etc.)

rules.title: Code Finder Rules
rules.description: Regex patterns to identify inline codes in translatable text

rules.title: Patterns
rules.description: List of regex patterns to detect inline codes

pattern.title: Pattern
pattern.description: Regex pattern for inline code detection

sample.title: Sample Text
sample.description: Sample text to test patterns against

useAllRulesWhenTesting.title: Test All Rules Together
useAllRulesWhenTesting.description: Test all rules together or individually

mergeAdjacent.title: Merge Adjacent Codes
mergeAdjacent.description: Merge consecutive inline codes into a single placeholder

moveBoundaryCodes.title: Move Boundary Codes
moveBoundaryCodes.description: Move inline codes at segment boundaries to the skeleton (non-translatable)

simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules
simplifierRules.description: Rules for simplifying inline code representation

useOkapiSegmentation.title: Use Okapi segmentation for output
useOkapiSegmentation.description:
If disabled, all XINI segments with the same value
of the attribute 'SegmentIDBeforeSegmentation' will be merged.
If the XINI was not segmented, it will remain unsegmented.
If this option is enabled, new segmentation
(i.e. from segmentation step) will be used for the output.

doc.overview: The XINI Filter reads and writes **XINI** documents — the XML interchange format used by the ONTRAM translation management system. A XINI document is organised as a set of pages, each containing elements (and tables of fields), whose text fields hold the translatable content as one or more segments. The filter walks this structure, emitting a group per page and a translatable text unit per field, and writes translations back into the corresponding XINI fields.

useOkapiSegmentation.help: Controls which segmentation is used when writing fields back to the XINI document. When enabled (the default), the segmentation produced by the pipeline (for example by a segmentation step) is used. When disabled, all segments sharing the same `SegmentIDBeforeSegmentation` attribute value are merged on output, restoring the document's original segmentation; a field that was not segmented in the input remains unsegmented. The bundled `okf_xini-noOutputSegmentation` configuration sets this to `false`.

processingNotes: Document structure maps to events as: each XINI page becomes a group, each element/field becomes a translatable text unit, and field segments become the text-unit segments.
Output is XML-encoded; the filter uses the XML encoder for the `text/x-xini` media type.

A predefined `okf_xini-noOutputSegmentation` configuration (i.e. `useOkapiSegmentation` set to `false`) merges segments that share the same `SegmentIDBeforeSegmentation` value in the output, reproducing the document's original segmentation rather than the pipeline's.
The filter supports an inline code finder and simplifier rules for handling placeholders within field text.

examples.title: Preserve the original XINI segmentation on output
examples.description: Disables Okapi segmentation so that fields are written back with their original XINI segmentation. Segments sharing the same `SegmentIDBeforeSegmentation` are merged; an originally unsegmented field stays unsegmented.

doc.overview: The XINI RainbowKit Filter is a variant of the XINI Filter for **XINI documents packaged as Rainbow translation kits** (ONTRAM T-Kits). It reads a XINI document and, when used to merge a kit, emits only the pages whose page name matches the original document being reconstructed, so each source document recovers its own content from a kit that may bundle several documents. It complements the standard XINI filter within the Rainbow translation-kit (T-Kit) round-trip rather than serving as a general-purpose XINI reader.
limitations: This filter is intended for the Rainbow T-Kit workflow (kit creation and merging of XINI files), not for ad-hoc reading of arbitrary XINI documents — for that, use the `okf_xini` filter.
It exposes no configurable parameters.
processingNotes: When merging a kit, the reader selects pages by matching each page's name against the relative document name being reconstructed, so unrelated pages in a multi-document kit are skipped.
A target locale is taken from the input document's target locales and used when reading the kit.
Output is XML-encoded using the XML encoder for the `text/x-xini` media type.
The filter has no configurable parameters; behaviour is fixed.

examples.title: Reconstructing a document from a T-Kit
examples.description: When merging a Rainbow translation kit, the filter reads the packaged XINI and yields only the pages whose page name corresponds to the document being reconstructed.
examples.input:
```
A T-Kit XINI containing pages for several source documents.
```
examples.output:
```
Only the pages whose page name matches the requested document are emitted as translatable content; pages belonging to other documents in the kit are skipped.
```

entries.description: Read and write OASIS XLIFF 1.2 bilingual exchange documents.

schema.title: XLIFF 1.2
schema.description: Configuration for the native XLIFF 1.2 bilingual exchange format reader/writer

addAltTrans.title: Add alt-trans elements
addAltTrans.description: Allow addition of new alt-trans elements

addTargetLanguage.title: Add target-language attribute
addTargetLanguage.description: Add the target-language attribute to file elements if not already present

allowEmptyTargets.title: Allow empty targets
allowEmptyTargets.description: Allow target elements with empty content to be written

alwaysAddTargets.title: Add targets for monolingual XLIFF
alwaysAddTargets.description: Add target elements for monolingual XLIFF files that have no target in the source

codeFinderRules.title: Code finder rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

editAltTrans.title: Edit alt-trans elements
editAltTrans.description: Allow editing of existing alt-trans elements

fallbackToID.title: Use trans-unit ID as name if no resname
fallbackToID.description: Use the trans-unit id attribute as the block name when no resname attribute is present

forceUniqueIds.title: Enforce unique trans-unit IDs
forceUniqueIds.description: Rewrite trans-unit IDs to enforce uniqueness across the file

ignoreInputSegmentation.title: Ignore input segmentation
ignoreInputSegmentation.description: Ignore seg-source/mrk segmentation from the input XLIFF and treat the full source as a single segment

includeExtensions.title: Include XLIFF extension elements
includeExtensions.description: Include non-standard namespace extension elements in extracted content

includeIts.title: Include ITS markup
includeIts.description: Include ITS (Internationalization Tag Set) markup in extracted content

overrideTargetLanguage.title: Override target language
overrideTargetLanguage.description: Override the target-language attribute in the output document with the pipeline target locale

preserveSpaceByDefault.title: Preserve whitespace by default
preserveSpaceByDefault.description: Treat all content as xml:space="preserve" unless explicitly overridden

targetStateValue.title: Target state value
targetStateValue.description: State value to set on target elements when useTranslationTargetState is enabled

useCodeFinder.title: Enable inline code detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection in translatable text

useTranslationTargetState.title: Update target state attribute
useTranslationTargetState.description: Update the state attribute on target elements when writing translated XLIFF

doc.overview:
XLIFF 1.2 is the OASIS XML Localisation Interchange File Format — the
bilingual exchange format that translation tools and TMS platforms use
to move content between extraction and translation. A document groups
`<trans-unit>` elements under `<file>` containers, each unit holding a
`<source>` and an optional `<target>`, with inline markup represented
through paired (`bpt`/`ept`, `g`) or standalone (`x`, `ph`) code
elements and segmentation expressed via `<seg-source>` and
`<mrk mtype="seg">`.

The reader emits one block per translatable `<trans-unit>`, carrying
the source, any existing target, segmentation, alt-trans matches, and
unit metadata. The writer updates targets and, by default, the target
`state` attribute, so a round-tripped file reflects translation
progress. This mirrors Okapi's XLIFF 1.2 filter.

addAltTrans.description: Allow new alt-trans elements to be added.
addAltTrans.help:
When enabled, new `<alt-trans>` elements (for example, machine or
memory matches) may be written into units. When disabled (the
default), no new alt-trans elements are added.

addTargetLanguage.description: Add the target-language attribute to file elements if missing.
addTargetLanguage.help:
When enabled (the default), the `target-language` attribute is added
to `<file>` elements that lack one, using the pipeline target locale.
When disabled, the attribute is left as found in the input.

allowEmptyTargets.description: Allow target elements with empty content to be written.
allowEmptyTargets.help:
When enabled, `<target>` elements may be written with empty content.
When disabled (the default), empty targets are not emitted. Enable it
when downstream tooling expects a `<target>` placeholder to exist
even before translation.

alwaysAddTargets.description: Add target elements for monolingual XLIFF that has none.
alwaysAddTargets.help:
When enabled, `<target>` elements are added for monolingual XLIFF
inputs that contain only sources. When disabled (the default), no
targets are synthesised for source-only files.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in content.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within translatable text is
protected as an inline code rather than translated. Only takes effect
when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.
examples: `%[0-9]*[sd]` — protect `printf`-style format specifiers

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

editAltTrans.description: Allow editing of existing alt-trans elements.
editAltTrans.help:
When enabled, existing `<alt-trans>` (alternative translation /
match) elements may be modified on write. When disabled (the
default), they are preserved unchanged.

fallbackToID.description: Use the trans-unit id as the block name when no resname is present.
fallbackToID.help:
When enabled, a `<trans-unit>` with no `resname` attribute takes its
`id` attribute as the block name, so the block still has a
human-meaningful identifier. When disabled (the default), the block
name is left empty when `resname` is absent.

forceUniqueIds.description: Rewrite trans-unit ids to enforce uniqueness across the file.
forceUniqueIds.help:
When enabled, duplicate `<trans-unit>` `id` values are rewritten so
every id is unique across the file. When disabled (the default), ids
are left as-is. Enable it for inputs whose ids are not guaranteed
unique and where downstream tooling requires uniqueness.

ignoreInputSegmentation.description: Treat the full source as one segment, ignoring input segmentation.
ignoreInputSegmentation.help:
When enabled, `<seg-source>`/`<mrk mtype="seg">` segmentation in the
input is ignored and the whole `<source>` is treated as a single
segment. When disabled (the default), the existing segmentation is
honoured and each `<mrk>` segment becomes its own segment in the
block.

includeExtensions.description: Include non-standard namespace extension elements in extracted content.
includeExtensions.help:
When enabled (the default), elements from non-XLIFF namespaces
(vendor or tool extensions) found within content are kept in the
extracted block. When disabled, such extension elements are dropped
from the extracted content.

includeIts.description: Include ITS (Internationalization Tag Set) markup in extracted content.
includeIts.help:
When enabled (the default), ITS markup carried in the XLIFF (e.g.
translate, locale-filter, or terminology annotations) is preserved
in the extracted content. When disabled, ITS markup is omitted.

overrideTargetLanguage.description: Replace the target-language attribute with the pipeline target locale.
overrideTargetLanguage.help:
When enabled, the `target-language` attribute in the output is
overwritten with the pipeline's target locale even if the input
already declared one. When disabled (the default), an existing
`target-language` is preserved.

preserveSpaceByDefault.description: Treat all content as `xml:space="preserve"` unless overridden.
preserveSpaceByDefault.help:
When enabled, every element is treated as whitespace-preserving
regardless of an explicit `xml:space` attribute. When disabled (the
default), the reader honours per-element `xml:space` only, collapsing
insignificant whitespace elsewhere.

targetStateValue.description: The state value written to target elements.
targetStateValue.help:
The XLIFF state value applied to `<target>` elements when
`useTranslationTargetState` is enabled. The default is
`needs-translation`. Set it to a standard XLIFF 1.2 state such as
`translated`, `needs-review-translation`, or `final` to match your
workflow.
examples: `translated`
`final`

dependsOn.condition: applied only when target state updating is enabled

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside content using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes in translatable text,
using the patterns in `codeFinderRules`. This supplements the native
XLIFF inline elements. When disabled (the default), only the XLIFF
inline elements are recognised.

useTranslationTargetState.description: Update the target `state` attribute when writing translated XLIFF.
useTranslationTargetState.help:
When enabled (the default), the writer sets the `state` attribute on
`<target>` elements to reflect translation status, using
`targetStateValue`. When disabled, the existing `state` is left
untouched.

processingNotes: One block is emitted per translatable `<trans-unit>`; units marked `translate="no"` are passed through as non-translatable.
Inline markup (`bpt`/`ept`, `g`, `x`, `ph`) and `<seg-source>`/`<mrk mtype="seg">` segmentation are read into the block.

examples.title: Mark targets as translated
examples.description: Write a final target state instead of the default needs-translation.

examples.title: Flatten input segmentation
examples.description: Treat each source as one segment, ignoring seg-source markers.

doc.overview: The Auto-XLIFF Filter is a meta-filter that detects the XLIFF version of the input document and delegates parsing to the appropriate XLIFF filter. It inspects the root element of the document: if the first element is `<xliff>` in the XLIFF 2.0 namespace with a `version` attribute in the range `[2.0, 3.0)`, the document is handled by the XLIFF 2.0 filter; otherwise it is handled by the XLIFF 1.x filter. This removes the need to know in advance whether a file is XLIFF 1.2 or 2.0, which is useful when processing mixed sets of XLIFF documents.

general.xliff2Config.help:
Names the filter configuration used to parse documents detected as XLIFF 2.0. The meta-filter selects this path when the root element is `<xliff>` in the XLIFF 2.0 core namespace with a `version` attribute in `[2.0, 3.0)`.

The value is a filter configuration id resolved by the bridge's configuration mapper. Use it to substitute a tuned XLIFF 2.0 configuration for the default.
general.xliff2Config.values: A filter configuration id (string). Default `okf_xliff2` — the standard XLIFF 2.0 configuration.

general.xliffConfig.help:
Names the filter configuration used to parse documents detected as XLIFF 1.x. When the root element is not XLIFF 2.0 (the fallback path), the meta-filter instantiates this configuration and hands the document to it.

The value is a filter configuration id resolved by the bridge's configuration mapper, not an inline parameter set. Use it to substitute a tuned or vendor-specific XLIFF 1.x configuration for the default.
general.xliffConfig.values: A filter configuration id (string). Default `okf_xliff` — the standard XLIFF 1.x configuration.
examples: `okf_xliff` — standard XLIFF 1.x handling (default)
`okf_xliff-sdl` — an SDLXLIFF-oriented configuration, if registered

processingNotes: Version detection reads only the document's first start element (root). The XLIFF 2.0 path is chosen when the root is `<xliff>` in the XLIFF 2.0 core namespace and its `version` attribute parses to a value `>= 2.0` and `< 3.0`. Any other root, namespace, or version (including a missing or unparseable `version`) falls back to the XLIFF 1.x filter.
Encoding is auto-detected from the byte-order mark; absent a BOM, UTF-8 is assumed.

Once a delegate filter is selected, all extraction and writing is performed by that filter using the configuration named by `xliffConfig` or `xliff2Config`. The parameters and behaviour of the chosen filter therefore apply in full.
Recognised file extensions are `.xlf`, `.xliff`, `.xlf2`, and `.xliff2`.

examples.title: Default auto-detection
examples.description: With no configuration, the filter detects the XLIFF version of each document and delegates to the default `okf_xliff` (1.x) or `okf_xliff2` (2.0) configuration accordingly.
examples.input:
```xml
<xliff version="2.0" xmlns="urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:document:2.0">
  <file id="f1"><unit id="u1"><segment><source>Hello</source></segment></unit></file>
</xliff>
```
examples.output:
```
The root <xliff> element is in the XLIFF 2.0 namespace with version 2.0, so the document is parsed by the XLIFF 2.0 filter (okf_xliff2).
```

examples.title: Selecting alternative delegate configurations
examples.description: Points the two version paths at non-default filter configurations — for example, a custom XLIFF 1.x configuration tuned for SDLXLIFF — so that auto-detection still applies but each path uses a tailored configuration.
examples.input:
```xml
<xliff version="1.2"><file source-language="en"><body><trans-unit id="1"><source>Hello</source></trans-unit></body></file></xliff>
```
examples.output:
```
The XLIFF 1.2 document is delegated to the okf_xliff-sdl configuration rather than the default okf_xliff.
```

entries.description: Read and write OASIS XLIFF 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 bilingual exchange documents.

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection in translatable text

version.title: XLIFF version
version.description: Target XLIFF 2.x version to emit. Empty means: preserve the input document version on roundtrip, otherwise default to 2.2.

writeOriginalData.title: Include original data in output

doc.overview:
XLIFF 2.x is the modern OASIS Localization Interchange File Format. It
replaces the 1.2 element soup with an explicit `<unit>`/`<segment>`
structure, file-scoped notes, a small set of inline tag elements
(`ph`, `pc`, `sc`/`ec`, `mrk`), and per-segment translation states. The
reader accepts the 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 namespaces as one compatible
family.

The reader emits one block per translatable unit and preserves inline
markup as raw inner XML so unmodified content round-trips byte-for-byte.
The writer can emit any 2.x version: by default it preserves the input
document's version on round-trip and falls back to 2.2 for content
extracted from a non-XLIFF source. This mirrors Okapi's XLIFF 2 filter.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in content.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within translatable text is
protected as an inline code rather than translated. Only takes effect
when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.
examples: `%[0-9]*[sd]` — protect `printf`-style format specifiers

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

discardInvalidTargets.description: Discard targets that fail validation instead of rejecting the file.
discardInvalidTargets.help:
When enabled, a `<target>` that fails validation is dropped while the
rest of the file is still processed. When disabled (the default), an
invalid target rejects the file. Enable it to salvage usable content
from partially invalid input.

forceUniqueIds.description: Ensure inline tag ids are unique within each unit.
forceUniqueIds.help:
When enabled, inline tag `id` values are rewritten so each is unique
within its `<unit>`. When disabled (the default), ids are left as-is.
Enable it for inputs whose inline ids may collide and where strict
validators require per-unit uniqueness.

ignoreTagTypeMatch.description: Ignore tag-type mismatches between source and target segments.
ignoreTagTypeMatch.help:
When enabled, a mismatch between the inline tag types in the source
and the target segment is tolerated rather than treated as an error.
When disabled (the default), the tag types are expected to match.

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside content using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes in translatable text,
using the patterns in `codeFinderRules`. This supplements the native
XLIFF 2 inline tag elements. When disabled (the default), only the
native inline elements are recognised.

version.help:
Selects the output XLIFF version. Leave it empty (the default) to
preserve the input document's version on round-trip and to fall back
to 2.2 for content with no source version. Set `2.0`, `2.1`, or `2.2`
to force a specific version; 2.0 and 2.1 share the
`urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:document:2.0` namespace, while 2.2
introduces `urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:document:2.2`.
examples: `` — auto: preserve input version, default 2.2
`2.0` — force XLIFF 2.0 output

writeOriginalData.description: Include original data in the output when available.
writeOriginalData.help:
When enabled (the default), the `<originalData>` carried by inline
tags is written to the output, preserving the original markup that
placeholders stand in for. When disabled, original data is omitted
from the output.

processingNotes: One block is emitted per translatable unit; inline markup is preserved as raw inner XML for byte-faithful round-trips.
The 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 namespaces are accepted as a compatible family on read.

examples.title: Force XLIFF 2.0 output
examples.description: Emit 2.0 regardless of the input document version.

examples.description: Drop targets that fail validation rather than rejecting the file.
